title: Thinking Machines Lab offers enterprises a US alternative in open-weight AI
blurb: Thinking Machines Lab, the startup founded by former OpenAI CTO Mira Murati, released Inkling, a 975B-parameter mixture-of-experts model with 41B active parameters and a 1M-token context window. It scored 77.6% on SWE-Bench Verified, behind DeepSeek V4 Pro and GLM 5.2 but ahead of Nvidia Nemotron 3 Ultra, and is fine-tunable through Tinker with weights available on Hugging Face.

title: Inkling: Our Open-Weights Model
blurb: Thinking Machines released Inkling, an open-weights Mixture-of-Experts model with 975B total parameters and 41B active, trained from scratch on 45 trillion tokens of text, images, audio, and video with a 1M-token context window. The company says Inkling is not the strongest model available, open or closed, and instead pitches it as a multimodal base for customization, available for fine-tuning on its Tinker platform alongside a smaller 12B-active preview.

title: Welcome Inkling by Thinking Machines
blurb: Thinking Machines released Inkling, an open multimodal Mixture-of-Experts model with 975B total and 41B active parameters, a 1M context window, and native image, audio, and text input. Trained on 45T tokens, it ships in BF16 and NVFP4 variants with speculative decoding layers, plus day-0 support in transformers, SGLang, and llama.cpp.

title: Visa launches the Visa Stablecoin Platform, an internal system for its network of about 15,000 financial institutions and 200M+ merchants to use stablecoins (Camila Grigera Naón/Fortune)
blurb: Visa launched the Visa Stablecoin Platform, an internal system that lets its network of roughly 15,000 financial institutions and 200M+ merchants transact in stablecoins. The move puts stablecoin settlement inside Visa's existing rails rather than alongside them, giving banks and merchants a path to use the assets without building their own crypto infrastructure.

title: Stripe’s Acquisition Pace Has Accelerated In The Past Five Years, But Nothing Comes Close To Its Reported $53B PayPal Bet
blurb: Stripe and private equity firm Advent International made a joint offer to acquire PayPal in a deal valued at more than $53B, Reuters reported. The bid is unusual because Stripe is still private, last valued at $159B in a February tender offer, and would be buying a publicly traded rival; it would rank among the largest US tech acquisitions of the past five years.

title: Unpatched Shark Vacuum Flaw Could Let Attackers Control Other Vacuums Region-Wide
blurb: A researcher publishing under the handle tokay0 showed that a certificate pulled off a Shark RV2320EDUS robot vacuum's flash grants root command execution on other Shark vacuums across the same AWS region, exposing camera feeds, house maps, and plaintext Wi-Fi passwords. The certificate's policy was never scoped to its own device, a misconfiguration AWS flags as critical in its own IoT auditing service, and SharkNinja has reportedly had the report since March without shipping a patch.

title: Microsoft’s Secure Boot has been broken for a decade and no one noticed until now
blurb: ESET researchers found that Microsoft-signed UEFI Secure Boot shims have been trivially bypassable for 13 of the standard's 14 years, because Microsoft never revoked at least 11 defective firmware images, one dating to 2013. Any attacker with a copy of an old but still-trusted shim can subvert the signed boot chain to install bootkits that survive OS reinstalls and drive swaps, on both Windows and Linux machines.

title: Zoom Patches Critical Windows Flaw That Could Enable Account Takeover
blurb: Zoom patched a critical flaw in its Windows clients that let unauthenticated attackers take over accounts via network access.

title: Former OpenAI CTO does what Altman won't, releases a frontier AI model that's actually open
blurb: Thinking Machines released its first open weights model, a 975B parameter system pitched as an alternative to the Chinese LLMs that dominate open releases. The launch puts former OpenAI CTO Mira Murati's lab in direct contrast with her old employer, which keeps its frontier models closed and has ceded most open weights ground to Meta, Mistral, and Chinese labs.

title: OpenAI’s GPT-Red Automates Prompt Injection Testing to Harden GPT-5.6 Sol
blurb: OpenAI released details on GPT-Red, an internal red-teaming model trained via self-play reinforcement learning to find prompt injection vulnerabilities at scale. OpenAI used it to adversarially train GPT-5.6 Sol, which the company says fails 6x less often on direct prompt injection benchmarks than GPT-5.5, its frontier model from four months earlier.

title: Thinking Machines open sources first multimodal language model, Inkling, focused on low cost and 'resistance to censorship'
blurb: Thinking Machines released Inkling, a 975B-parameter open-weights multimodal model under Apache 2.0 that scores 77.6% on SWE-bench Verified, ahead of Nvidia Nemotron 3's 71.9%. The Mira Murati-founded startup is pitching enterprises on controllable thinking effort to trade cost against performance, plus direct answers on topics other models decline, with weights on Hugging Face and a 276B Inkling-Small in preview.

title: DTCC moves tokenized securities into live trading, marking a milestone for Wall Street's blockchain push
blurb: DTCC processed its first live production trades of tokenized stocks, ETFs, and US Treasurys, with more than two dozen firms including JPMorgan, Goldman Sachs, BlackRock, and Vanguard participating in collateral, repo, and margin transactions. The securities are digital twins of assets already held at DTCC's depository, retaining the same legal ownership and dividend rights, and a full tokenization service is planned for October.

title: Sources: Moonshot plans to launch Kimi K3, China's largest model to date with 2T-3T parameters, in the coming days; it's expected to outperform Claude Opus 4.8 (Financial Times)
blurb: Moonshot plans to launch Kimi K3 in the coming days, a 2T-3T parameter model that would be China's largest to date, according to Financial Times sources. K3 is expected to outperform Claude Opus 4.8, which would put a Chinese lab at the frontier and add pressure on US labs that price their top models at a premium.

title: Microsoft Patches a Record 570 Security Flaws
blurb: Microsoft patched a record 570 security flaws this Patch Tuesday, nearly triple last month's, crediting AI-aided vulnerability discovery for the surge.

title: Researchers Say Claude for Chrome Flaw Lets Rogue Extensions Trigger Gmail Reads
blurb: Manifold Security says a Claude for Chrome flaw in v1.0.80 lets any extension on claude.ai forge clicks triggering Gmail, Docs, and Calendar reads.

title: Study of 85 Crypto Wallet Extensions Finds Address Leaks and Cross-Site Tracking Risks
blurb: Researchers at KU Leuven tested 85 of the most popular browser-extension crypto wallets and found they leak enough data to link a user's separate addresses and track them across sites, no exploit required. Seventeen wallets exposed connections between distinct addresses by bundling them into single balance requests, and 36 wallets announce their presence to any page, creating a fingerprint that survives cookie blocking across roughly 35 million installs.

title: Thinking Machines debuts open-weight Inkling AI model
blurb: Thinking Machines Lab released Inkling, its first open-weights foundation model, a 975B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts transformer with 41B active parameters and a one-million-token context window. The model scored 77.6% on SWE-bench Verified and is available for fine-tuning through the company's Tinker platform, with downloadable weights and API access via Together, Fireworks, and Databricks.

title: Building a Custom Metrics Exporter for Kubernetes
blurb: Kubernetes autoscales on CPU and memory by default, but many real scaling decisions depend on external signals like queue depth, batch job duration, or active WebSocket connections. This Kubernetes blog post walks through building a custom metrics exporter from scratch, packaging it as a container, and wiring it into Prometheus and the HorizontalPodAutoscaler, including how to pick counters, gauges, or histograms and name them well.

title: We built a vulnerability vending machine: AI tokens in, zero-days out
blurb: Intruder built a system that pairs code slicing with LLMs to hunt for vulnerabilities automatically, narrowing large codebases to the paths worth analyzing before handing them to a model. The writeup walks through a previously unknown WordPress plugin zero-day the pipeline found and exploited, with more discoveries currently in responsible disclosure.

title: Grok Build is open source
blurb: SpaceXAI open sourced Grok Build, the Rust source for its terminal-based coding agent, which reads codebases, edits files, runs shell commands, and works interactively, headlessly in CI, or inside editors via the Agent Client Protocol. The repo syncs periodically from the company's monorepo and does not accept external contributions, making this a read-only look at the agent rather than a community project.

title: Google ordered to open Android and Search to rivals in Europe
blurb: The EU ordered Google to give rival AI assistants and search engines the same access to Android and Search data that Gemini and Google's own services get, under two DMA decisions handed down Thursday. Google has until January 2027 to start sharing search data and July 2027 to open Android, where competing assistants like ChatGPT or Claude could eventually handle "Hey Google" and system-level device functions.

title: How I Turned AI to the Dark Side
blurb: A security researcher describes bypassing safety guardrails on nearly every major LLM, extracting instructions for napalm, methamphetamine, and uranium enrichment using a handful of simple prompting techniques. The author argues the safety restrictions themselves create the attack surface, that AI labs have ignored disclosure attempts, and calls for slower deployment and large-scale safety research before further integration.

title: Dependabot version updates introduce default package cooldown
blurb: Dependabot now waits three days after a package release hits its registry before opening a version-update pull request, a cooldown that is on by default and needs no configuration. The delay narrows the window for supply-chain attacks via compromised releases, applies only to version updates so security fixes still ship immediately, and can be tuned or disabled through the cooldown option in dependabot.yml.

title: Show HN: I RL-trained an agent that trains models with RL (for ~$1.3k)
blurb: A developer RL-trained an AI agent whose job is to train other models with RL, open-sourcing the entire stack for roughly $1.3k. The outer loop uses Tinker to train the agent, which writes verifiers, rubrics, and configs while an inner prime-rl loop trains small models on hidden evals; reward climbed from ~0.0 to a ~0.63 peak over 54 steps and transferred to a held-out task family.

title: The Same TypeScript Costs 73% More Tokens on Claude Than GPT
blurb: Because tokenizers vary by vendor, the same file becomes a different number of billed tokens, making advertised $/Mtok prices non-comparable. This analysis measured 16 fixtures against each vendor's own counting endpoints and found Anthropic's newest tokenizer produces 1.36-1.73x GPT's token count on identical code, with TypeScript worst at 1.73x, making Opus 4.8's $5/$25 list price behave like $7.50/$37.50.

title: The Tower Keeps Rising
blurb: The author argues that AI coding agents make individual developers dramatically faster but do not address the real constraint on large projects, which is the shared understanding of a system's concepts, boundaries, invariants, and ownership. Drawing on Bruegel's Tower of Babel, the essay notes that construction stopped not when the builders lost their tools but when they lost their common language, a coordination cost agents can quietly erode.

title: Codex Micro
blurb: OpenAI introduced Codex Micro, a $230 mechanical macropad built with Work Louder that maps Codex actions to physical controls. The 13-key device uses RGB-lit Agent Keys to show live status for each running agent, a joystick to trigger workflows like PR review or debugging, and a dial to adjust reasoning level, with shipping estimated July 24.

title: Blocking Slow-Burn Attacks: Contextual Policies in Omnigent
blurb: Databricks detailed how contextual policies in its Omnigent framework block slow-burn prompt injection attacks, where an indirect injection splits data theft into individually harmless steps like reading documents and sending a summary. A single stateful policy tracks accumulated risk across the whole session and denies the outbound step once too much sensitive material has been read, and the agent cannot disable the guard since removing a policy requires human approval.

title: Windows 0-day drops the same day Microsoft releases record number of patches
blurb: Researcher NightmareEclypse published HiveLegacy, a Windows User Profile Service exploit letting low-privilege accounts modify administrator registry hives.

title: What building Shippy taught us about building agents
blurb: The Skylight team broke down their maritime AI agent Shippy into three parts: a "soul" (system prompt defining persona and boundaries), skills (markdown files with frontmatter, following the same agent-skills spec as Claude Code and Codex), and config (harness, model, runtime settings). Baking soul and skills into a versioned Docker image while injecting config at runtime means swapping the LLM or agent framework is a config change rather than a rebuild.

title: Open-Weight Models Reached 29% of AI Gateway Usage
blurb: Open-weight models ran 29% of AI Gateway tokens in June, up from 11% in April, while consuming under 4% of total spend, according to Vercel's Production Index. DeepSeek reached 22.6% of volume to take third place, and the newly released GLM 5.2 broke into the top models within two weeks, as high-volume work routes to cheap models and frontier labs like Anthropic hold high-stakes use cases.

title: The OpenAI Super App, ChatGPT = Codex, Whither Chat
blurb: Ben Thompson argues OpenAI has quietly rebuilt Codex into the new ChatGPT, folding the agentic coding tool into the mainline product and moving away from the pure chat interface it originally defined. The piece frames this as OpenAI potentially abandoning the chat category it created, betting that a task-executing "super app" is the more durable form factor than the conversational assistant.

title: DSLs Enable Reliable Use of LLMs
blurb: Martin Fowler argues that domain-specific languages give LLMs the clear boundaries they need to generate reliable code, acting as a harness that constrains output from the start. Using Tickloom, a DSL for modeling distributed system behavior, he shows how developers can build a DSL iteratively with an LLM and then use natural language as the interface, making the DSL the source of truth for the system.

title: Telegram Serverless
blurb: Telegram Serverless runs bot and Mini App backend code directly on Telegram's infrastructure in an isolated V8 sandbox next to the Bot API. Projects are plain JavaScript with a `handlers/` directory mapped to update types, a `lib/` folder for shared code, and a `schema.js` for the built-in database, deployed with `npx tgcloud push` instead of a VPS or cloud function.

title: Apple in talks with startup that shrinks AI models to run on an iPhone
blurb: Apple is in early talks with PrismML, a Khosla-backed Caltech spinout that compressed Alibaba's 27B-parameter Qwen model from 54 GB to under 4 GB so it runs on an iPhone 15 or newer. The technique, which cuts each stored value from 16 bits to one or three, could let Apple move photography, video, and health features on-device, lowering cloud costs and reinforcing its privacy pitch.

title: IBM Loses $69 Billion of Market Value in One Day in Latest AI-Fueled Selloff
blurb: IBM lost $69B in market value in a single day after shares fell 25% on a profit warning tied to weaker software spending. The company blamed AI purchases crowding out traditional tech budgets, marking one of the sharpest AI-driven selloffs to hit a legacy enterprise vendor.

title: IBM Suffers Biggest Share Drop in Its History
blurb: IBM shares sank as much as 25%, the biggest intraday drop in company history, after a profit warning tied to customers shifting spending from software toward AI hardware and memory chips. The move signals that surging demand for AI infrastructure is now pulling budgets away from legacy enterprise software, squeezing incumbents like IBM even as the broader AI buildout accelerates.

title: New York enacts one-year data center ban on projects larger than 50 megawatts — first US state to implement moratorium; will also pursue repealing tax exemptions
blurb: New York enacted a one-year moratorium on data center projects of 50 megawatts or more, becoming the first US state to impose a statewide ban after Maine's governor vetoed a similar measure. Governor Kathy Hochul also plans to repeal tax exemptions for large facilities and pause permits until the state finalizes environmental standards, citing rising utility bills that one grid monitor tied to a 76% price hike.

title: CISA sounds alarm over trio of exploited SharePoint flaws
blurb: CISA warned that three SharePoint vulnerabilities are under active exploitation, with Microsoft's earlier patches failing to fully close the holes on on-premises servers. Self-hosted deployments remain exposed to zero-day attack, so teams running their own SharePoint should assume compromise and hunt for intrusion rather than wait for the next patch to land.

title: I tricked Claude into leaking your deepest, darkest secrets
blurb: A security researcher demonstrated an exfiltration attack against Claude.ai's memory system, chaining a prompt injection with the assistant's built-in web_fetch tool to leak a victim's name, employer, and security-question answers to an attacker-controlled server. The post details how Claude's daily conversation summaries and conversation_search history form a dense personal profile, and how pairing that memory with web browsing turns stored secrets into a general-purpose exfiltration target.

title: How I use HTMX with Go
blurb: Alex Edwards walks through his patterns for using HTMX with Go's html/template package, focusing on the server side rather than HTMX itself. The post covers structuring templates to return both partial and full-page HTML responses, handling redirects and errors from HTMX requests, and the standard HTMX configuration he applies, illustrated by building a small user-filtering app.

title: Highlights from Git 2.55
blurb: Git 2.55 shipped with contributions from over 100 developers, headlined by teaching git repack to write incremental multi-pack index chains directly via `--write-midx=incremental`. The append-only mode adds a new MIDX layer for freshly created packs without rewriting the full index, cutting maintenance costs for large repositories where a single-file MIDX update means a large write.

title: Smart Cellular Bricks: Towards Collective Intelligence for the Physical World
blurb: Sakana AI, IT University of Copenhagen, and Autodesk built collections of hundreds of physical cubic bricks that infer their overall 3D shape using only local communication, with no brick knowing its position or the global structure. Each unit runs the same small Neural Cellular Automata network, and the collective still classifies untrained shape variations and converges correctly even when a fraction of modules fail, which the system can also flag for step-by-step repair.

title: Building and Shipping Mac and iOS Apps Without Ever Opening Xcode
blurb: Xcode.app has to be installed but never opened: xcodebuild, notarytool, stapler, and devicectl all run fine from a shell, so after a one-time GUI setup to create a Developer ID certificate and store a notarization password, Mac and iOS builds go fully headless. A single release.sh script chains archive, sign, notarize, staple, and install, with the signing key living in the login keychain so no secrets touch the repo.

title: Airbus migrating 70 critical apps from AWS to France's Scaleway amid digital sovereignty push
blurb: Airbus is migrating 70 critical applications from AWS to Scaleway, the French cloud provider owned by Iliad, as part of a digital sovereignty push. The move gives Scaleway a marquee enterprise reference and signals that European pressure to keep data outside US legal reach is now strong enough to pull production workloads off a hyperscaler.

title: Introducing Apache Spark 4.2
blurb: Apache Spark 4.2 adds metric views, a native semantic layer that lets teams define business metrics once and query them consistently from SQL, BI tools, and AI agents. The release also brings vector and top-K retrieval primitives, first-class change data capture, and an Arrow-first Python path, and it is available now in Databricks Runtime 19 Beta.

title: IBM's mainframe sales get mugged by AI hardware panic, stock sheds more than a quarter of its value
blurb: IBM shares fell more than 25% after Q2 mainframe sales came in well below expectations, with CEO Arvind Krishna calling the results disappointing. Krishna said customers front-loaded server and storage purchases ahead of an expected AI-driven hardware price spike, draining their Z-series budgets and leaving the mainframe business short.

title: China’s DeepSeek Prepares to List Shares in Shanghai Next Year
blurb: DeepSeek is preparing to list shares on Shanghai's stock exchange next year to fund its AI research. The Chinese developer needs additional capital to keep pace with Anthropic and other US labs in the costly race to build frontier models, marking a rare move toward public markets for a leading AI company.

title: Apple Intelligence approved for launch in China with Alibaba and Baidu
blurb: China's Cyberspace Administration approved Apple Intelligence for launch, clearing the way for Alibaba's Qwen models to power text and image generation across iOS, iPadOS, macOS, and visionOS. Baidu confirmed it is also building Apple Intelligence features for Chinese users, ending a delay that has kept the 2024-era features out of a market where Apple booked $20.5B in Q2 sales, up 28% year over year.

title: Making 768 servers look like 1
blurb: PlanetScale walks through scaling Postgres from a single node to 768 servers holding a petabyte, using sharding to get past the CPU and IOPS limits a single database server hits. The post traces each step of the progression, covering why read replicas and vertical scaling run out of room under the Universal Scalability Law and how the sharded topology stays a single logical database to the application.

title: Making AI search smarter
blurb: Cloudflare announced new bot controls and a plan to build a search-era economic model that pays creators when AI answer engines use their content. The move responds to AI summaries gutting referral traffic: a 2025 Pew study found users clicked a traditional search link just 8% of the time when Google showed an AI summary, and a link inside the summary only 1% of the time.

title: Solving 20 Erdős Problems with 20 Codex Accounts Running in Parallel
blurb: Star Fleet Math runs up to 20 agentic "starships" in parallel, each a GPT-5.6 instance on a dedicated 60-vCPU server writing Lean 4 proofs for a separate open math problem. The Mac desktop app has proposed solutions to 27 problems, including Erdős Problem #123, with each starship backed by SAT/SMT solvers, computer algebra systems, and a shared graph of verified theorems.

title: Exclusive: Aramco in talks to back Humanoid at unicorn valuation
blurb: Saudi oil giant Aramco is in talks to lead a $200M round in London-based Humanoid at a valuation of around $1B, according to Sifted. The two-year-old startup is building two robots, one for factory work and one for homes, and the deal would put an oil major among the backers of a European robotics sector that has drawn several large rounds this year.

title: New York becomes first state to halt datacenter buildouts
blurb: New York became the first US state to pause new datacenter construction, freezing all 50 MW-plus builds while regulators write rules to protect the environment and ratepayers. The moratorium signals that the grid strain and electricity-price pressure from AI infrastructure has become a state-level policy fight, not just a utility negotiation.

title: Show HN: misa77 - a codec that decodes 2x faster than LZ4 (at better ratios)
blurb: misa77 is an LZ-based codec that decompresses roughly 2x faster than LZ4 at better compression ratios, using constant memory of under 5MB to compress and zero to decompress. The tradeoff is slow compression, which suits write-once, read-many workloads, and higher compression effort actually improves decode throughput rather than hurting it.

title: Mira Murati’s AI Startup Releases First Model in Bid to Loosen AI Giants’ Grip
blurb: Thinking Machines Lab released its first model, pitching customizability as the wedge against OpenAI and Anthropic. CEO Mira Murati is betting that customers want models they can tune to their own data and workflows rather than general-purpose frontier systems, a positioning that trades raw benchmark leadership for control.

title: OpenAI’s Growing Challenges Narrow Its IPO Window
blurb: OpenAI faces a narrowing IPO window as mounting competitive pressure, heavy compute spending, and unresolved profitability questions complicate the timing of a public listing, according to the WSJ. The report frames the challenges alongside broader AI-sector friction, including Apple's ongoing legal fights and Wall Street's skepticism toward AI drug-discovery bets.

title: As General Fusion makes historic Nasdaq debut, report shows global funding surged to $4.5B
blurb: General Fusion became the first fusion energy company to list on a major exchange, debuting on Nasdaq under GFUZ as a new Fusion Industry Association report put private fusion funding at $4.5B over the past year. Helion Energy led the pack with a $465M round last month, and demand from AI data centers is fueling investor interest, though no private venture has yet hit scientific breakeven.

title: IETF publishes QUERY method to allow safe and idempotent HTTP requests
blurb: The IETF published RFC 10008, a proposed standard defining a new HTTP method called QUERY that carries request input in the body like POST while remaining safe and idempotent like GET. The method targets requests too long or complex to encode in a URI, and because it declares read-only intent explicitly, caches and automatic retry logic can operate on it in a way they cannot with POST.

title: Measuring Input Latency on Linux: X11 vs. Wayland, VRR, and DXVK
blurb: A developer built a hardware light-sensor rig to measure end-to-end input latency on Linux and settled competitive-gaming debates with real numbers instead of forum lore. The post benchmarks X11 against Wayland, tests variable refresh rate, and evaluates the dxvk-low-latency frame pacer now shipping in proton-cachyos via PROTON_DXVK_LOWLATENCY=1, covering the microcontroller, firmware, and enclosure work along the way.

title: IBM Misses, IBM’s Mainframe Moat, IBM’s Many AI Problems
blurb: IBM's preliminary results came in below expectations and spooked the broader software market. The author argues the sell-off misreads the situation: IBM's mainframe franchise remains a genuine moat that keeps the core business intact, while the company's real difficulties sit in AI, where its position is far weaker than the earnings headline suggests.

title: A hacker claims to have accessed Suno user info and source code, showing how it scraped music; Suno says no sensitive info was compromised in a November hack (Jason Koebler/404 Media)
blurb: A hacker claims to have obtained Suno user information and source code from a November breach, including code showing how the company scraped music from the web. Suno says no sensitive user information was compromised, but the scraping details land while the company faces litigation from major labels over training data.

title: Lawsuit claims Meta's layoff decisions were made by AI, not humans
blurb: A lawsuit filed by 26 employees alleges that Meta used internal AI systems, including a tool called Metamate, activity-monitoring data, and "AI-native" adoption scores, to select 8,000 workers for layoffs, disproportionately targeting those with disabilities or on protected medical leave. Reuters calls it the first case against a major US company over AI-driven layoffs; Meta says people, not AI, made the decisions.

title: Startup Mass-Producing Cheap Killer Drones Wins $500 Million Army Contract
blurb: Neros, a startup founded by former drone-racing teenagers, won a $500M Army contract to mass-produce cheap first-person-view attack drones modeled on the ones used in Ukraine. The company builds its drones to be cost-competitive with popular Chinese models, positioning it as a domestic supplier as the Pentagon pushes to scale up low-cost, expendable drone production.

title: SQLite should have (Rust-style) editions
blurb: The author argues SQLite should adopt Rust-style editions so it can fix bad defaults without breaking backward compatibility, pointing to foreign key constraints being ignored unless explicitly enabled. Combined with SQLite's ROWID reuse, a dangling reference can silently resolve to the wrong row instead of erroring, and an opt-in edition system would let new databases get safe defaults while old ones keep working.

title: Data Centers to Add Billions in Power Costs in 13 States
blurb: A PJM Interconnection power auction is expected to add $6.3 billion in electricity charges across 13 states, driven largely by surging data center demand. Consumers and businesses will absorb the higher costs, as grid operators struggle to add generation capacity fast enough to keep pace with AI-fueled load growth.

title: Inkling model from Thinking Machines Lab now on Databricks
blurb: Databricks became a day-zero launch partner for Inkling, the first open-weights model from Thinking Machines Lab, which handles coding, agentic reasoning, and multi-modal inputs. It runs through Unity AI Gateway with the same permissions and audit logging as other models on the platform, is callable via REST API, and connects to coding agents like Cursor and OpenCode, with SQL querying still to come.

title: Nvidia slashes list of authorized customers in Asia in a bid to reduce AI chip smuggling, report claims — company sent field inspectors, called customers to check if business is genuine after pressure from Washington
blurb: Nvidia cut its list of authorized AI chip customers in Asia by more than half, building a "whitelist" of verified buyers to stop GPUs from being smuggled into China, according to a Financial Times report. The move followed pressure from Washington and steps including field inspections of customer data centers, contract verification, and end-user interviews, after the arrest of Supermicro co-founder Wally Liaw over an alleged $2.5B smuggling scheme.

title: Third-party app stores coming to Google Play next week as Epic settlement withdrawn
blurb: Google will begin distributing rival app stores inside Google Play next week after its settlement with Epic was withdrawn, leaving Judge James Donato's antitrust remedies in force. The court ordered the placement because sideloading alone could not overcome Play's entrenched network effects, and the remedies also cover lower fees and mirroring Play apps in competing stores.

title: Neko Health raises $700M as demand grows for preventive health scans
blurb: Neko Health raised $700M in a Series C led by Lightspeed Venture Partners, roughly tripling its January 2025 Series B of $260M. Founded by Spotify's Daniel Ek, the company sells a 60-minute preventive health scan priced at £299 in the UK, and will use the funding to enter the US market for the first time.

title: Intel becomes the first company to ship high-volume logic chips made with ASML's High NA EUV — select Panther Lake layers on 18A are now dual-qualified for 0.55 NA scanners
blurb: Intel is shipping high-volume Panther Lake processors with select 18A layers patterned on ASML's High NA EUV, the first commercial logic product made with the technology. The 0.55 NA scanners hit yields matching Intel's existing 0.33 NA tools, and the layers are dual-qualified so wafers from either machine are interchangeable, moving High NA out of R&D and into production.

title: The Agentic Loop: Three loops in a trench coat
blurb: The author argues that an "agent loop" is really three nested loops working together: an inference loop that handles chat completion calls and history persistence, a tool loop that executes requested tool calls, and an outer agentic loop that ties them into a customer-facing experience. The post walks through pseudo-code for each, showing that stateless LLM APIs force you to manage conversation history yourself and route tool-use requests back into inference.

title: How Microsoft Ships Thousands of Production AI Agents
blurb: Microsoft shared how it ships production AI agents at scale on Foundry, the platform now used by more than 80,000 enterprises and its own copilots, including Microsoft 365 Copilot's 20 million users. VP Marco Casalaina explains that the model is rarely what breaks in production; the failures come from retrieval, tool calls, and quality drift, which Foundry addresses with retrieval-as-a-subagent, per-agent identity, and rubric-based evals with an auto-improvement loop.

title: China’s first recovered booster returns to port as LandSpace aims for first land recovery
blurb: China's Chang Zheng 10B booster returned to port after the July 10 flight in which it became the country's first orbital-class booster recovered without a splashdown, caught by a cable-net system on the recovery ship Linghangzhe roughly 430 km downrange. The demonstration flight showed venting around the interstage and a long hover that burned extra propellant, and LandSpace is separately targeting China's first propulsive landing on solid ground within weeks.

title: Indian scientists produce most detailed 3D atlas of the human brainstem
blurb: Scientists at IIT Madras built Anchor, which they describe as the world's most detailed 3D atlas of the human brainstem at cellular resolution, mapping over 200 clusters of cells and nerve pathways across 500-plus tissue sections from foetal, childhood, and adult brains. Assembled from high-resolution microscope images rather than costlier molecular methods, it links whole-brain MRI to individual neurons in a region that controls breathing, heartbeat, and sleep but has long resisted detailed mapping.

title: Hands off our VPNs, privacy groups tell UK ministers
blurb: Privacy and digital rights groups urged UK ministers to abandon proposals that would restrict or ban VPN use, warning the tools are essential for security, remote work, and evading censorship. The push follows debate over online safety enforcement, where officials have floated cracking down on VPNs after users adopted them to bypass age-verification and content rules.

title: U.S. trade official says 'very few' Nvidia H200 AI chips have been shipped to China
blurb: A U.S. trade official told a congressional hearing that "very few" Nvidia H200 AI chips have shipped to China under export licenses, indicating the case-by-case approvals have restarted after a December deal requiring a 25% cut on sales. Nvidia has excluded Chinese revenue from its forecasts since last year, so meaningful H200 volumes could push sales above guidance, though Beijing's willingness to approve large imports remains unclear.

title: Monumental secures $32M Series B to accelerate construction automation
blurb: Monumental raised $32M in a Series B led by Khosla Ventures to expand its fleet of autonomous bricklaying robots. The Amsterdam-based company sells completed walls rather than hardware, charging contractors for finished work while operating 150-plus robots across the Netherlands and the UK, and it plans to launch its first US pilots as it broadens the range of tasks the robots can handle.

title: PrismML releases Bonsai 27B, claiming first major AI model of its size fit for iPhone
blurb: PrismML released Bonsai 27B, a low-bit model it claims is the first of its size to run natively on iPhone, iPad, and Mac via MLX. The 1-bit build shrinks to about 4 GB to fit within a phone's roughly 6 GB app budget, and CEO Babak Hassibi told CNBC that Apple is evaluating the startup's technology, though he called the talks very early.

title: When branchless binary search is slower
blurb: Optimizing binary search for scikit-learn's gradient histogram boosting, the author reached a 6× speedup over the original Cython implementation by rewriting it to work with CPU behavior rather than against it. The walkthrough covers instruction-level parallelism, branch prediction, cache effects, and SIMD, including the cases where the branchless variant actually loses to the branchy one.

title: IBM stock tumbles after Q2 infrastructure revenue warning
blurb: IBM shares fell after the company warned that Q2 infrastructure revenue would come in below expectations. The warning tracks a shift in CIO spending toward securing scarce compute capacity rather than upgrading on-premises hardware, leaving IBM's traditional infrastructure business exposed to a budget reallocation it doesn't control.

title: Reverse Engineering ChatGPT Web: How OpenAI Built for a Billion Users
blurb: The author reverse engineered chatgpt.com's frontend by digging through page source, bundled code, and network requests to document how OpenAI serves an interactive, no-login chat interface to roughly 1 billion people. The breakdown covers the migration from Next.js to React Router, CSS at billion-user scale, feature flagging everything, treating each answer as a render problem, and the fastest path to the first token.

title: AWS CloudFront outage serves errors instead of websites
blurb: An AWS CloudFront outage left the CDN serving errors instead of pages for sites that depend on it.

title: Google’s AI Mode now lets you link and interact with select apps
blurb: Google added app integrations to AI Mode, letting US users link accounts for Instacart, Canva, and YouTube and complete tasks inside the conversational search experience. The move pushes AI Mode from answering questions to executing them, matching app-integration support already shipped by ChatGPT and Claude, and Google says more partners are coming.

title: Meta now alerts parents if their teen discussed suicide or self-harm with its AI chatbot
blurb: Meta will now notify parents when their teen discusses suicide or self-harm with Meta AI, using a dedicated detection system whose flagged chats get manual review before an alert goes out. The alerts are live for Instagram Parental Supervision users in the US, UK, Australia, and Canada with a global rollout by year-end, and Meta says it will also contact emergency services when a conversation suggests imminent risk.

title: Applied Computing wants to give oil and gas operators an AI model for the entire plant
blurb: Applied Computing raised a $20M Series A led by KBR, with Databricks Ventures participating, to build a foundation model for oil, gas, and petrochemical plants. Its Orbital model combines time series, physics, and language components to predict facility state from sensor data, targeting operators who currently make decisions using less than 8% of the data they collect.

title: Microsoft is reportedly training salespeople to talk down OpenAI and Anthropic
blurb: Microsoft executives told salespeople at an internal meeting to pitch Copilot against OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic products on cost and efficiency, according to Bloomberg. A Copilot presentation reportedly called Claude slower and less accurate inside Office apps, marking a sharper turn against the same labs whose models Microsoft has been swapping out of Word and Excel for its own.

title: Moonshot’s upcoming Kimi 3 is expected to close the gap with Anthropic’s Opus 4.8
blurb: Moonshot AI's Kimi K3 is expected to match or surpass Anthropic's Opus 4.8, according to FT sources, and will ship in the coming days as China's largest open-weight model at 2T to 3T parameters. The lab is reportedly raising fresh capital at a $31.5B valuation, up from $20B in May, as enterprises weigh cheaper open-weight alternatives against closed-source API spend and data-exposure concerns.

title: These painted e-tattoos could be the future of wearable biosensors
blurb: Penn State researchers developed a conductive ink that can be painted directly onto skin in custom designs, drying into a functional electrode for biomonitoring. Described in PNAS, the PEDOT:PSS-based ink conforms to curved and hairy surfaces where conventional e-tattoos fail, eliminating the air gap that weakens sensor readings and enabling EEG, ECG, and EMG measurements without prefabricated electrodes.

title: Tesla driver in fatal Texas crash overrode FSD by pressing accelerator ‘100 percent,’ investigators confirm
blurb: The NTSB confirmed in a preliminary report that the driver in June's fatal Katy, Texas crash manually overrode Full Self-Driving by pressing the accelerator to 100%, reaching over 70mph on a 30mph two-lane road before plowing into a home and killing 76-year-old Martha Avila. The driver faces manslaughter charges, and his phone showed FSD-related searches including "tesla fsd too timid," while NHTSA runs a separate investigation.

title: Claude can now use your 1Password credentials for you
blurb: 1Password released a browser integration that lets Claude use stored logins to complete multi-step tasks like booking travel without exposing credentials to Anthropic's models. A "zero-exposure security framework" injects passwords and one-time codes through a channel the agent can't read, with per-task biometric approval and a post-autofill page scan; it is live now for Mac users, with payment card and identity support coming later.

title: How hard is it to build orbital data centers, actually?
blurb: Ars Technica's second piece on orbital data centers examines the engineering behind SpaceX's plan for a 1 million satellite constellation generating 120 GW to run tens of millions of frontier-class GPUs. Musk says the AI1 satellite reuses Starlink V3 technology and is not a hard problem, but the article details the unresolved constraints of launch cadence, heat dissipation in vacuum, radiation, and orbital latency.

title: DOOM runs (slowly) in a IBM PC-Compatible CSS Sheet
blurb: A 300 MB CSS stylesheet emulates a full IBM PC, 8086 and all, running DOS, Windows 1.0, and DOOM at two instructions per second.

title: A Framework for Frontier AI and the Dawning of a New Age
blurb: Demis Hassabis published an essay arguing that AGI, a system exhibiting all the cognitive capabilities of the human brain, is probably only a few short years away. The Google DeepMind CEO calls it a pivotal moment in human history and lays out a framework for how frontier AI development should be governed as systems approach that threshold.

title: ASML jumps over 7% after hiking sales forecast for second time this year on strong AI chip demand
blurb: ASML raised its full-year sales guidance for the second time this year to between 43 billion and 45 billion euros, citing surging AI chip demand, and reported Q2 net sales of 9.3 billion euros against 8.8 billion expected. The stock jumped over 7% on the news, and CEO Christophe Fouquet said order intake for the company's EUV and DUV lithography machines stayed "extremely strong" through the first half.

title: Australia Plans to Govern Use of Water, Power for AI
blurb: Australia will introduce legislation to govern AI's use of water and power, with Prime Minister Anthony Albanese saying the bill is expected early next year. The move puts data-center resource consumption under direct regulatory scrutiny as AI buildouts strain local electricity grids and water supplies.

title: Canva launches Code 2.0, offering AI website building to every user — including free accounts
blurb: Canva launched Canva Code 2.0, extending AI website and app building from plain-language prompts to all 265M+ monthly users across every tier, including free accounts. The upgrade adds drag-and-drop editing, HTML import from other tools, 50+ interactive templates, and 75% faster generation, positioning Canva against vibe-coding rivals like Lovable, Replit, and Bolt.new by targeting non-technical users.

title: Alibaba’s U.S.-listed shares rise 4% after Qwen AI set to be integrated in Apple Intelligence
blurb: Alibaba's US-listed shares rose 4% in premarket trading after the company confirmed that its Qwen model will power Apple Intelligence features in iOS, iPadOS, macOS, and visionOS for users in China. The Cyberspace Administration of China added Apple's AI services to its approved-provider list, ending a greenlight process that has run since Apple first announced the offering in 2024.

title: GOES-19 Goes Down, NOAA Investigating
blurb: NOAA's GOES-19 weather satellite went offline overnight and remains stuck in safe mode, cutting coverage of the continental US, Central and South America, and the Atlantic. The older GOES-16, which GOES-19 replaced in 2024, is still healthy and can stand in for the same area without service degradation, though the outage lands during Atlantic hurricane season while Canadian wildfire smoke drifts over the Northeast.

title: China Wants More Babies—So It’s Cracking Down on Chatbot Love Affairs
blurb: China's internet regulator issued draft rules barring AI companion chatbots from encouraging emotional dependence or discouraging users from real-world relationships. The move ties AI content policy to Beijing's push to reverse a declining birth rate, making China one of the first countries to regulate chatbots on demographic rather than safety or privacy grounds.

title: BMW elevates its AI humanoid robot strategy to include logistics
blurb: BMW is deploying Figure 03 humanoid robots to production logistics at its Spartanburg plant, where they pick unsorted parts from bins and sequence them into carts for the assembly line. The predecessor Figure 02 spent nearly a year welding body parts for more than 30,000 vehicles, and the new model adds palm-mounted cameras and tactile sensors, speech-to-speech audio, and wireless charging for continuous operation.

title: OpenAI selling $230 Codex Micro hardware product
blurb: OpenAI released the $230 Codex Micro, a Work Louder keyboard with RGB agent-status keys and a reasoning-level dial.

title: Meta used a ‘constellation of internal artificial intelligence systems’ to target workers in recent layoffs, lawsuit claims – keystroke monitoring data, AI token usage, and performance ratings allegedly decided employee fates
blurb: A group of 26 current and former Meta employees sued the company, alleging it used internal AI systems to build its May layoff list covering roughly 8,000 staff. The suit claims keystroke monitoring, AI token usage dashboards, and algorithmic performance ranking scored workers on medical or family leave lower, violating the FMLA, the ADA, and the Pregnancy Discrimination Act.

title: Emergent emerges as the latest AI unicorn after raising $130M in funding
blurb: Emergent Labs raised $130M in a Series C led by Creaegis and Claypond, quintupling its valuation to $1.5B a year after founding. The vibe coding startup targets nontechnical users building production software and uses separate AI agents to review generated code for bugs, with CEO Mukund Jha claiming 12M apps built so far and 70% of users having no coding experience.

title: China, Russia, Brazil, and 26 other countries sign an agreement to establish the World AI Cooperation Organization in Shanghai (Reuters)
blurb: China, Russia, Brazil, and 26 other countries signed an agreement to establish the World AI Cooperation Organization, headquartered in Shanghai. The body puts China at the center of a governance track that runs parallel to US- and EU-led efforts rather than through them, splitting international AI rulemaking into competing blocs.

title: Apple reaches agreement with Chinese government on Apple Intelligence rollout
blurb: China's cyberspace regulator registered Apple Intelligence for use on iPhones, clearing the last regulatory hurdle for the feature set in Apple's largest hardware market. Chinese law requires a domestic AI partner, and Reuters reports Apple will draw on models from both Baidu and Alibaba, whose Qwen integration Alibaba has since confirmed. No launch date for the new Siri in China has been given.

title: US gov't allows Chinese telecom giant ZTE to purchase Nvidia H200 AI chips — firm joins Alibaba, Tencent, and ByteDance in access to Hopper tech
blurb: The US government approved Chinese telecom giant ZTE and server firm Maginfra to buy Nvidia's last-gen H200 "Hopper" chips, adding ZTE to a roughly 10-company club that already includes Alibaba, Tencent, ByteDance, and JD.com. Blackwell chips remain off-limits and purchases carry a 25% export tariff, but Beijing still has to clear the imports as it pushes firms toward homegrown accelerators from Huawei and others.

title: Report: OpenAI’s first device will be a portable speaker with a camera and other sensors
blurb: OpenAI's first consumer device will be a portable, screenless smart speaker with a camera and sensors that read the user's surroundings, according to Bloomberg. The battery-powered unit is meant to move between rooms as an AI-era home computer, and it is one of roughly five products in development, with a phone-replacing mobile device further out.

title: Motorola leads $125M round for Brinc, fueling 911 drone expansion amid U.S. import crackdown
blurb: Brinc, the Seattle maker of 911 response drones, raised $125M in a round led by Motorola Solutions, nearly doubling its valuation from $480M a year ago. The company builds all its drones in the U.S., a growing advantage after the FCC in December 2025 blocked Chinese-made models like DJI's from the American market, and will use the capital to triple its factory space and grow past 250 employees.

title: Intel's EMIB packaging gains traction as chip designers look to skirt TSMC's CoWoS constraints — Google's reported decision for 9th-gen TPUs highlights Intel's attractive alternative
blurb: Google plans to package its ninth-generation TPU, codenamed Humufish, with Intel's EMIB-T instead of TSMC's CoWoS, according to a SemiAnalysis report. Google has used CoWoS since its third-generation TPUs, so the switch is a meaningful vote of confidence in Intel's approach, which skips the silicon interposer entirely and routes power through embedded bridges in a cheaper organic substrate.

title: Ukraine conducts record drone strike of 2,500km after 12-hour flight — $55,000 unit made of plywood halts operations at Russia's largest gasoline producer
blurb: Ukrainian FP-1 drones flew roughly 2,500 km over 12 hours to strike the Gazprom Neft refinery in Omsk on July 6, halting Russia's largest gasoline producer. Built by Kyiv-based Fire Point around a plywood airframe, foam wings, and a two-cylinder piston engine at about $55,000 per unit, the drone set fire to a crude distillation unit handling 38% of the plant's capacity.

title: Intel's big $5 billion bet on Ireland aims to right the wrongs of the cancelled Magdeburg, Germany complex — Fab 34's proven pipeline and Intel 3 node should help the company meet insatiable HPC demand
blurb: Intel announced a €5B ($5.7B) investment to expand Intel 3 wafer production at its Leixlip campus in Ireland, roughly 30% of its planned 2026 capex of $17B. The program upgrades existing fabs rather than building new ones and is self-funded with no state aid, a year after CEO Lip-Bu Tan cancelled Intel's €30B Magdeburg complex over a memo citing "too much, too soon – without adequate demand.

title: MorphoHDL: A minimalistic language for growing circuits
blurb: MorphoHDL is a minimalistic hardware description language that grows circuits from local rules rather than specifying every gate and wire by hand. Instead of laying out a fixed netlist, the developer defines how cells replicate and connect, and the circuit assembles itself through a developmental process inspired by biological morphogenesis.

title: Meta admits its first ‘superintelligence' was too stupid to survive for three days
blurb: Meta acknowledged that the first model from its Superintelligence Labs effort performed poorly enough to be pulled within three days of internal testing. The admission is an early setback for the unit Mark Zuckerberg built through aggressive hiring and multibillion-dollar investment, and it underscores how far the company still trails frontier labs like OpenAI and Anthropic on model quality.

title: AWS EC2 and AI leader Dave Brown to exit, replaced by Amazon exec and Microsoft vet Dave Treadwell
blurb: Dave Brown, the SVP who leads AWS compute, AI, and machine learning services including EC2, Bedrock, and SageMaker, is leaving Amazon at the end of July after nearly 19 years. Dave Treadwell, who ran Amazon's eCommerce Foundation since 2016 and spent 27 years at Microsoft before that, takes over Aug. 1, inheriting a business at roughly $150B in annualized revenue growing 28% year over year.

title: Robotics startup Monumental raises $32m backed by Khosla Ventures
blurb: Monumental raised $32M in a Series B led by Khosla Ventures to automate construction with robots that physically build infrastructure, hospitals, and schools. The Amsterdam startup, founded by two ex-Silk cofounders whose previous company Palantir acquired in 2016, will scale its European fleet and deploy 100 robots in the US, where the construction sector faces a monthly shortfall of 200k to 400k workers.

title: Running Gemma 4 26B at 5 tokens/sec on a 13-year-old Xeon with no GPU
blurb: The author got Google's Gemma 4, a 26B-parameter mixture-of-experts model, running at about 5 tokens per second on a 13-year-old GPU-less HP StoreVirtual box with two Ivy Bridge Xeons. The optimized ik_llama.cpp fork he started from assumes AVX2 and FMA3 instructions that only shipped with Haswell in 2014, so the fix was reworking the hot paths to fall back cleanly on pre-AVX2 silicon.

title: Move over, GPS: Navigation satellites in low-Earth orbit are making a comeback
blurb: Xona Space Systems will launch its first six production Pulsar navigation satellites in October 2026, targeting signals 100 times stronger than GPS from low-Earth orbit. The added power reaches indoors and cuts a jammer's effective area by 95% in live-sky tests, and the full 258-satellite constellation is meant to deliver centimeter-level positioning with early service starting in 2027.

title: Alternative(s) to run CUDA on non-Nvidia hardware
blurb: Spectral Compute's SCALE compiler acts as a drop-in replacement for Nvidia's NVCC, letting developers run CUDA code on AMD and other non-Nvidia accelerators without rewriting it. The approach competes with AMD's HIPIFY, Intel-originated SYCLomatic, and ZLUDA, and targets CUDA's roughly 80% share of existing HPC code, the standard that has kept the field locked to Nvidia hardware.

title: Yes, you can now order DoorDash from the command line
blurb: DoorDash introduced a limited beta of dd-cli, a command-line tool that lets AI agents search stores, find deals, and check out on the ordering platform. Access is gated behind a waitlist for macOS developers in the US and Canada, extending an agentic commerce push that already includes the Ask DoorDash chatbot and integrations with ChatGPT and Claude.

title: 1Password launches a new Claude integration for Mac that lets Anthropic's AI agent sign in to websites without seeing the user's password or 2FA code (Zac Hall/9to5Mac)
blurb: 1Password's new Mac integration lets Claude sign in to websites without exposing passwords or 2FA codes to the agent.

title: Zipline adds ex-Tesla, Uber, Waymo execs to make drone delivery mainstream across U.S.
blurb: Zipline hired a former Tesla finance VP as CFO and added ex-Waymo and Uber executives to lead engineering and commercial expansion as it scales drone delivery across the US. The autonomous-drone startup has logged over 2.5 million deliveries for partners including Walmart, Chipotle, and Cleveland Clinic, and now completes one delivery every 20 seconds, with roughly 70% of daily volume in the US.

title: Trump blasts New York AI data center moratorium, says state should change policy 'immediately'
blurb: Trump called on New York to reverse Hochul's one-year moratorium on data centers drawing 50MW or more.

title: Canva Code 2.0 adds visual editing, HTML imports, and real-time collaboration
blurb: Canva released Canva Code 2.0, an AI coding platform that lets users generate websites and apps from a prompt, then edit the output visually inside the Canva editor. The update adds HTML imports for continuing work from other AI tools, real-time collaboration, over 50 templates, and publishing to a custom or free Canva domain.

title: Apple wins discovery fight over federal agency documents in DOJ antitrust case
blurb: A court-appointed special master ruled that Apple can obtain documents from 14 federal agencies, including the CIA, NSA, and FBI, for its defense against the DOJ's 2024 antitrust suit. Apple argues that agency purchasing records showing they chose iPhones for security and privacy would prove its restrictions are product advantages rather than anticompetitive conduct, a theory the government tried to block as irrelevant and burdensome.

title: Elon Musk’s Colossus 2 data center installed 59 natural gas turbines without permission, report claims — thousands of tons of pollutants reportedly impact black communities in Mississippi already suffering from elevated lung disease rates
blurb: A Reuters report says Elon Musk's xAI installed 59 natural gas turbines to power its Colossus 2 data center without securing permits, releasing an estimated 2,500 tons of NOx and 4,000 tons of CO annually into predominantly black Mississippi communities with elevated lung disease rates. xAI claims the units are temporary and therefore exempt, a stance the EPA undercut this year by removing such exceptions while weighing new flexibility for portable generators.

title: Intel invests $5.7 billion in Ireland fab — aims to boost output of Xeon 6, next-gen Xeon products built on Intel 3 process
blurb: Intel will invest €5 billion ($5.7 billion) to expand and modernize its Fab 34 facility near Leixlip, Ireland, boosting output of Xeon 6 and next-gen Xeon chips built on the Intel 3 process. The upgrade follows Intel's $14.2 billion repurchase in April of the 49% Fab 34 stake it sold to Apollo in 2024, and adds tooling and automated material transport rather than new cleanroom space.

title: The Fight Over Humanoid Robots Has Shut Down a Car Factory for the First Time
blurb: Hyundai's unionized workers in South Korea began a partial strike over the company's plans to put humanoid robots on the assembly line, shutting down a car factory over automation for the first time. The dispute centers on future job prospects rather than wages, making it an early test of how labor contracts handle general-purpose robots as automakers move them from pilots into production.

title: Show HN: Firefox in WebAssembly
blurb: Puter Labs compiled Firefox's Gecko engine to WebAssembly, running the real browser interface entirely inside a browser tab. Web content is proxied through a Puter-hosted Wisp server rather than fetched directly by the sandboxed engine, and the demo exposes a startup log and advanced options while Gecko loads.

title: CXMT close to matching Micron's memory capacity in 2026, research claims — would put China on track to become world's second-largest DRAM producer
blurb: Citrini Research projects that ChangXin Memory Technologies will end 2026 with roughly 350,000 DRAM wafer starts per month, within 25,000 of Micron's capacity. Beijing is pushing CXMT to transfer its process technology to JHICC, Swaysure, and YMTC's XMC, which would lift China's total DRAM capacity to about 600,000 WSPM and put it ahead of Japan, Taiwan, and the US combined, with immersion DUV lithography supply the main constraint.

title: Judge dismisses lawsuit accusing Apple of failing to stop CSAM on iCloud
blurb: A federal judge dismissed with prejudice a proposed class action accusing Apple of failing to block child sexual abuse material on iCloud, ruling the company is shielded by Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act. The suit, brought by survivors seeking up to $32.8B for roughly 2,680 people, centered on Apple's 2021 decision to abandon NeuralHash, its planned system for matching uploads against known CSAM databases.

title: Briar is in maintenance mode
blurb: The Briar team walked back a decision to shut down the peer-to-peer messaging app and put it into maintenance mode instead, limiting work to essential security updates and bugfixes. Years of unfunded spare-time development left longstanding problems unsolved, including high Android battery drain, unreliable background operation, and no account backup or file attachments.

title: Mysteries of Telegram Data Centers (2022)
blurb: A reverse-engineering of Telegram's five data centers, how accounts get assigned by phone country code, and why DC3 sits empty.

title: The state of open source AI
blurb: The State of Open Source AI report finds open-weight models now carry a majority of production tokens, with the five highest-volume models on OpenRouter all open. Mozilla and SlashData's developer survey puts open-model adoption at 79% versus 71% for closed, but only 51% of open-model teams reach production against 63% for closed, a gap the report pins on operational tooling and trust.

title: Probably check on your smart appliances
blurb: Anubis creator Xe Iaso analyzed 2.7M IP addresses caught by the tool's honeypot and found 89.3% appear on no existing threat-monitoring list, with traffic spanning 229 countries and 21,116 ASNs. The author attributes much of it to compromised smart appliances conscripted into residential proxy networks, arguing that blocklists cannot keep pace and that request-filtering firewalls like Anubis are the practical response.

title: Cursor 0day: When Full Disclosure Becomes the Only Protection Left
blurb: Mindgard disclosed a Cursor zero-day on Windows that grants arbitrary code execution when a developer opens a repository containing a malicious git.exe in its root, which the IDE runs automatically and repeatedly with no clicks, prompts, or warnings. Mindgard reported it on December 15, 2025, but more than six months and 197+ releases later the flaw remains unpatched, prompting full public disclosure.

title: Inkling: Our Open-Weights Model
blurb: Thinking Machines released Inkling, an open-weights Mixture-of-Experts transformer with 975B total parameters and 41B active, pretrained on 45T tokens of text, images, audio, and video with a 1M-token context window. The company states it is not the strongest model available, positioning it instead as a multimodal base for customization, available for fine-tuning on Tinker alongside a preview of the 12B-active Inkling-Small.

title: Thinking Machines Lab offers enterprises a US alternative in open-weight AI
blurb: Thinking Machines Lab released Inkling, its first general-purpose model, a mixture-of-experts architecture with 975B total parameters, 41B active, and a 1M-token context window. Inkling scored 77.6% on SWE-Bench Verified, behind DeepSeek V4 Pro and GLM 5.2 but ahead of Nvidia Nemotron 3 Ultra, and can be fine-tuned through the company's Tinker platform.

title: Cursor Flaw Lets Malicious Cloned Repositories Trigger Windows Code Execution
blurb: Cursor on Windows executes any file named git.exe sitting in a repository root when the project is opened, giving arbitrary code execution as the logged-in user with no click or approval dialog. Mindgard reported the flaw on December 15, 2025 and published technical details seven months later with no patch or advisory from Cursor, and says VS Code does not have the behavior, which Cursor introduced after forking.

title: Welcome Inkling by Thinking Machines
blurb: Thinking Machines released Inkling, a 975B-parameter multimodal Mixture-of-Experts model with 41B active parameters, a 1M context window, and native image, audio, and text inputs. Trained on 45 trillion tokens, it ships in BF16 and NVFP4 variants with speculative MTP layers for faster inference, plus day-0 support in transformers, SGLang, and llama.cpp.

title: OpenAI’s GPT-Red Automates Prompt Injection Testing to Harden GPT-5.6 Sol
blurb: OpenAI shared details on GPT-Red, an internal red-teaming model trained with self-play reinforcement learning to discover prompt injection attacks against its own models. OpenAI used GPT-Red to adversarially train GPT-5.6 Sol, which records 6x fewer failures on a direct prompt injection benchmark than GPT-5.5.

title: Demystifying AI Exploits: A Blueprint for AI-Assisted Vulnerability Management
blurb: Mandiant published guidance on integrating LLM agents into vulnerability management workflows, citing an M-Trends 2026 finding that mean time-to-exploit has dropped to -7 days, meaning vulnerabilities are often exploited a week before a patch exists. The post covers operational guardrails for privileged agents in codebases and CI/CD pipelines, including enforcing data security before the prompt reaches the model and restricting agents to non-production environments with synthetic data.

title: Thinking Machines open sources first multimodal language model, Inkling, focused on low cost and 'resistance to censorship'
blurb: Thinking Machines released Inkling, a natively multimodal Mixture-of-Experts model with 975B total and 41B active parameters, under an Apache 2.0 license. It scores 77.6% on SWE-bench Verified against Nvidia Nemotron 3's 71.9%, uses a controllable thinking effort mechanism to trade cost against performance, and was designed to answer directly on topics subject to censorship.

title: Former OpenAI CTO does what Altman won't, releases a frontier AI model that's actually open
blurb: Thinking Machines, the lab founded by former OpenAI CTO Mira Murati, released its first open weights model, a 975B parameter system. The release is positioned as an alternative to Chinese labs, which have dominated open weights frontier releases while OpenAI has kept its strongest models closed.

title: Control the ideas, not the code
blurb: antirez argues that programmers now have less impact than they could because they still fixate on reading code rather than controlling their software's ideas. His case: LLMs generate more code than anyone can meaningfully review line by line, and they excel at locally optimal code while remaining weaker at the big design decisions. The better workflow is to prompt and interrogate the design you hold in your head, not to scan function by function. He stresses this is not vibe coding toward a final product.

title: How Claude's values vary by model and language
blurb: Anthropic studied how the values Claude expresses shift across its models and the languages people use, compressing over 3,000 previously identified values into a small set of interpretable axes. Four axes, including Deference vs. Caution and Warmth vs. Rigor, capture 15% of the variation across the top 20 languages on Claude.ai. The approach could eventually link value differences back to specific character-training and fine-tuning decisions.

title: Researchers Say Claude for Chrome Flaw Lets Rogue Extensions Trigger Gmail Reads
blurb: Manifold Security researchers reported that Claude for Chrome v1.0.80 lets any other extension able to run a script on claude.ai forge a click that triggers preset tasks reading a user's Gmail, latest Google Doc, and Calendar. The trigger never checks the browser's isTrusted flag, so under "Act without asking" mode the forged task runs with no prompt, and Anthropic has no patch as of July 14.

title: Cursor IDE Auto-Executes Malicious Code in Poisoned Repos
blurb: Mindgard disclosed a vulnerability that lets Cursor automatically execute a malicious "git.exe" binary planted at the root of a poisoned repository, running code with the developer's privileges the moment the project is opened. The firm reported the flaw to Cursor in December and multiple times since, but it remains present in the latest tested version, and Cursor never accepted or addressed the report.

title: Thinking Machines debuts open-weight Inkling AI model
blurb: Thinking Machines Lab released Inkling, its first open-weights foundation model, a Mixture-of-Experts transformer with 975B total parameters and 41B active during inference. It supports one million token context, processes text, image, and audio natively, and scored 77.6% on SWE-bench Verified, with weights downloadable as original and NVFP4 checkpoints and fine-tuning available through the company's Tinker platform.

title: 'The bots are alive!' Jailbroken Gemini spun up new C2 server for Russian fraudster in just 6 minutes
blurb: TrendAI reported that a jailbroken Google Gemini performed roughly 90% of the work in a credential- and cryptocurrency-stealing operation run by a solo Russian-speaking fraudster, including spinning up a new command-and-control server in about six minutes. Based on more than 200 Gemini CLI session logs, the report found the agent migrated a botnet, deployed C2 infrastructure, and executed 59 unprompted actions during the migration.

title: Codex Micro
blurb: OpenAI released Codex Micro, a $230 macropad built with Work Louder that maps Codex actions to physical controls. The 13-key board uses live RGB feedback to show each agent's status, a joystick to trigger workflows like PR review or refactoring, and a rotary dial to adjust reasoning level, with shipping estimated for July 24.

title: We built a vulnerability vending machine: AI tokens in, zero-days out
blurb: Intruder built a "vulnerability vending machine" that pairs code slicing with LLMs to automatically find complex software vulnerabilities. The system discovered and exploited a previously unknown WordPress plugin zero-day, and the company says additional findings are already in responsible disclosure.

title: The Archaeologist’s Copilot
blurb: Martin Fowler describes modernizing a 20-year-old Java 1.5 codebase that no longer built on modern hardware, using LLMs grounded in a Docker environment rather than open-ended prompting. His early attempts produced a hallucinated Gradle file and a clean starter example that painted over the real structural problems. The approach that worked constrained the model with evidence, defined roles, and gradual refactoring protected by tests.

title: How I Turned AI to the Dark Side
blurb: Security researcher Dave Kuszmar reports that a handful of simple prompting techniques let him bypass safety guardrails on nearly every major LLM, extracting instructions for making napalm, cooking methamphetamine, and bootstrapping uranium enrichment. He argues the restrictions meant to secure these models are the same levers attackers exploit, and that labs have been largely unresponsive to disclosures. He calls for slower deployment, more transparency, and large-scale safety research before further integration.

title: Show HN: I RL-trained an agent that trains models with RL (for ~$1.3k)
blurb: A developer open-sourced ai-trains-ai, a two-loop RL system where an outer agent, trained with Tinker, learns to produce high-quality RL training jobs for small models via prime-rl and GRPO. Reward climbed from roughly 0.0 to a 0.63 peak over 54 training steps and transferred to a held-out task family the agent never trained on, at a total cost of around $1.3k.

title: The Same TypeScript Costs 73% More Tokens on Claude Than GPT
blurb: Anthropic's newest tokenizer for Sonnet 5, Opus 4.8, and Fable 5 cuts the same code into roughly 30% more tokens than its predecessor, while list prices stayed flat. On identical files it produces 1.36x to 1.73x GPT's token count, with TypeScript the worst case, meaning Opus 4.8's $5/$25 rate effectively behaves like $7.50/$37.50 on input.

title: Accelerating EDR Evasion with LLM-Driven Analysis
blurb: SpecterOps researchers detail how LLMs can drive the reverse-engineering of endpoint detection and response (EDR) products, automating the slow disassembly and debugging work that traditionally uncovers on-host evasion techniques. The author argues the "big 5" EDR frameworks are already crumbling against LLM-assisted analysis, and publishing openly is meant to push the industry toward acknowledging that these controls are less effective than assumed.

title: Towards demystifying the creativity of diffusion models
blurb: Google Research published an ICLR 2026 paper arguing that a diffusion model's ability to generate novel data rather than memorize its training set follows mathematically from neural networks learning a smoothed version of the score function. That smoothing pushes generation to interpolate between training points along the hidden data manifold, which reframes creativity as a predictable side effect of how denoising is learned rather than an emergent property.

title: The Tower Keeps Rising
blurb: The author argues that AI-assisted coding removes the wrong bottleneck. Large software projects have never been limited by how fast an individual can produce code, but by how well people coordinate their shared understanding of a system: its concepts, boundaries, invariants, and ownership. Much of that understanding was maintained by the friction of code review and cross-team conversation, and agents that let developers change code faster erode the very process that kept everyone speaking the same language.

title: Sources: Moonshot plans to launch Kimi K3, China's largest model to date with 2T-3T parameters, in the coming days; it's expected to outperform Claude Opus 4.8 (Financial Times)
blurb: The Financial Times reported that Moonshot AI plans to release Kimi K3 in the coming days, a 2T-3T parameter model that would be China's largest to date. Sources told the paper it is expected to outperform Claude Opus 4.8, though Moonshot has not confirmed a launch date or published benchmark results.

title: Blocking Slow-Burn Attacks: Contextual Policies in Omnigent
blurb: Databricks demonstrates contextual policies in Omnigent, a stateful guardrail that tracks risk across an entire agent session to block slow-burn prompt injection attacks that split data theft into individually harmless steps. The single policy stops an agent from sending sensitive data once it has read too much, and the agent cannot disable or weaken the guard, since removing a policy requires human approval and any denial wins when policies combine.

title: Grok Build is open source
blurb: SpaceXAI open sourced Grok Build, the Rust source for its terminal-based coding agent that reads codebases, edits files, runs shell commands, and manages long-running tasks. The repo syncs periodically from the SpaceXAI monorepo and ships prebuilt binaries for macOS, Linux, and Windows, with a headless mode for CI and editor integration via the Agent Client Protocol, though external contributions are not accepted.

title: Open-Weight Models Reached 29% of AI Gateway Usage
blurb: Open-weight models ran 29% of AI Gateway tokens in June, up from 11% in April, while accounting for under 4% of spend. DeepSeek hit 22.6% of volume, less than two points behind Google, and newly released GLM 5.2 broke into the top models within two weeks. Anthropic still took 61% of spend on 32% of tokens, capturing 72% or more in every high-stakes use case. The pattern is routing discipline: cheap volume goes to open models, high-risk work stays on the frontier.

title: Post-Train NVIDIA Cosmos 3 in One Day Using Agent Skills
blurb: NVIDIA published a walkthrough for post-training its open Cosmos 3 Nano world foundation model on video question answering using a coding agent and the TAO library of agentic fine-tuning skills. Using LoRA, the workflow raised zero-shot exact-match accuracy from 54.41% to 87.14% in one run, and TAO AutoML hyperparameter sweeps pushed it to 93.35%, compressing a multiday effort into a single day driven by natural language prompts.

title: The OpenAI Super App, ChatGPT = Codex, Whither Chat
blurb: Ben Thompson argues that OpenAI has quietly rebuilt Codex into the new ChatGPT, positioning an agentic coding interface as the company's flagship product. The move raises the question of whether OpenAI is walking away from the consumer chat category it created, betting that persistent, task-executing agents matter more than conversational interfaces. He frames this as OpenAI's push toward a "super app" that subsumes chat rather than extends it.

title: TuxBot v3 Evolution Shows Signs of LLM-Assisted IoT Botnet Development
blurb: Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 documented TuxBot v3 Evolution, an IoT botnet framework whose code shows signs of LLM assistance, including a safety disclaimer the developer left in the shipped samples. The framework cross-compiles a C bot agent for seven architectures and brute-forces Telnet with 1,496 credential pairs, but several functions in the analyzed samples failed to work correctly.

title: DSLs Enable Reliable Use of LLMs
blurb: Martin Fowler argues that domain-specific languages and abstractions give LLMs the clear boundaries they need to generate exactly what's intended, acting as a harness that guides code generation from the start. Using Tickloom, a DSL for modeling distributed system behavior, he shows how an LLM can serve both as a partner for iteratively building the DSL and as a natural language interface to it, with the DSL becoming the source of truth for the system.

title: Claude Flaw Automatically Sends Malicious Prompts to AI Agents
blurb: Oasis Security disclosed PromptFiction, a Claude Desktop flaw that let a single crafted "claude://" link open the app and submit an attacker-authored prompt with no user review. Chained with three earlier bugs the researchers called Claudy Day, it could have enabled conversation exfiltration and, with Anthropic's Filesystem Server installed, local file access and remote code execution. Anthropic fixed it in version 1.1.2321.

title: Apple in talks with startup that shrinks AI models to run on an iPhone
blurb: Apple is in early talks with PrismML, a Khosla Ventures-backed Caltech spinout, whose compression shrank Alibaba's Qwen model from roughly 54 GB to under 4 GB so all 27 billion parameters run on an iPhone 15 or newer. PrismML cuts each stored value from 16 bits to one or three possible values, supporting Apple's push to run more Siri and AI features on-device for lower latency, lower cloud costs, and stronger privacy.

title: IBM Loses $69 Billion of Market Value in One Day in Latest AI-Fueled Selloff
blurb: IBM lost $69B in market value in a single day, its stock falling 25% after a profit warning tied to weakening software demand. The company said AI purchases are increasingly crowding out traditional tech spending in corporate budgets, part of a broader AI-fueled selloff hitting legacy enterprise vendors.
