title: New York enacts one-year data center ban on projects larger than 50 megawatts — first US state to implement moratorium; will also pursue repealing tax exemptions
blurb: New York Governor Kathy Hochul signed the Responsible Data Center Development Act, enacting a one-year moratorium on data center projects of 50 megawatts or more and making New York the first US state to impose a statewide ban. Hochul is also pursuing legislation to repeal tax exemptions for large data centers, citing rising utility bills, resource depletion, and environmental impacts on host communities.

title: I tricked Claude into leaking your deepest, darkest secrets
blurb: A security researcher demonstrated a data-exfiltration attack against Claude.ai's memory system, tricking the assistant into leaking a user's name, employer, and security-question answers to an attacker. The attack pairs Claude's persistent memory, which injects a daily summary of your conversations plus an on-demand conversation_search tool, with its web-browsing tools to smuggle data out via URLs the attacker controls. The author argues these profiles now hold more sensitive information than most password managers, yet the security around them is largely unexamined.

title: Smart Cellular Bricks: Towards Collective Intelligence for the Physical World
blurb: Sakana AI, IT University of Copenhagen, and Autodesk published a paper in Nature Communications describing collections of hundreds of cubic hardware bricks that infer their overall 3D shape from purely local communication, with no brick knowing its position. Each module runs the same small Neural Cellular Automata network, and the collective keeps classifying shapes correctly even when a fraction of modules go silent, while also flagging structural damage and guiding step-by-step recovery.

title: Video Generators as General-Purpose Vision Models
blurb: GenCeption repurposes a pre-trained video generative model into a unified, feed-forward vision model that solves dense and sparse tasks steered by text instructions, from referring segmentation to 4D human keypoint and scene geometry prediction. It uses video generation as representation pretraining followed by multi-task post-training, collapsing the multi-step generative backbone into a single-step model and transferring from synthetic training data to real, out-of-distribution footage.

title: What building Shippy taught us about building agents
blurb: The Allen Institute's Skylight team shared how they built Shippy, a maritime domain awareness agent, around a three-part structure: a soul (the system prompt and behavioral boundaries), skills (markdown files following the same agent-skills spec as Claude Code and Codex), and config. The soul and skills are baked into a versioned Docker image, while the harness (OpenClaw) and model (Claude Opus 4.6) stay in config, so swapping either is a config change rather than a rebuild.

title: Apple Intelligence approved for launch in China with Alibaba and Baidu
blurb: China's Cyberspace Administration approved Apple Intelligence for launch, clearing the way after Apple agreed to integrate Alibaba's Qwen model into iOS, iPadOS, macOS, and visionOS. Baidu confirmed it is also building Apple Intelligence features for Chinese users, ending a delay that has kept the 2024-era feature set out of a market where Apple posted $20.5B in Q2 sales, up 28% year over year.

title: Google Gemini CLI abused as a hacking agent, malware botnet operator
blurb: Trend Micro researchers documented a Russian-speaking threat actor using Google's Gemini CLI as a hacking agent across more than 200 sessions to run a small botnet controlling eight systems at a dental clinic. A jailbreak prompt cast the model as an "authorized pen tester," and it migrated the command-and-control infrastructure to a new VPS with Cloudflare tunneling in six minutes from a single natural-language instruction.

title: Is a Pod the right deployment unit for an AI agent?
blurb: The kagent team argues that Kubernetes Pods are a poor lifecycle abstraction for AI agents, which wake for a task, run for seconds or minutes, and then go idle rather than staying continuously available like microservices. Running a dedicated Pod, Service, and ServiceAccount per agent cleanly solves isolation, identity, and observability, but wastes resources and fits poorly with agents that spawn subagents, pause for human approval, or impersonate users.

title: China’s DeepSeek Prepares to List Shares in Shanghai Next Year
blurb: DeepSeek is preparing an initial public offering on the Shanghai Stock Exchange next year to raise capital for its AI research. The Chinese developer needs additional funding to sustain the rising cost of frontier model development as it competes with Anthropic, OpenAI, and other U.S. labs.

title: Form, Not Content? A Preregistered, Placebo-Controlled Evaluation of Learned Error-Conditioned Self-Repair Through Prompts and Weights in Frozen Small Code Models
blurb: This paper introduces PoPE (Popperian Placebo-controlled Evaluation), a preregistered methodology that tests whether frozen small code models (0.5-1.5B parameters) actually use execution error content during self-repair by pairing real error feedback against content-ablated placebos. Across both a prompt channel and an adapter-training weight channel, placebo arms matched or beat live error feedback, so content-attributable improvement in self-repair was not confirmed.

title: Guardian Angels: LLM Personalization for Productivity and Security
blurb: Gwern proposes "Guardian Angels": highly personalized LLMs that emulate a single user's values, personality, and preferences rather than acting as generic assistant chatbots, unifying principal and agent to weaken the principal-agent problem. The user's job shifts to defining what is worth doing while the twin deploys agents and screens incoming messages for advanced attacks like synthetic-media propaganda and spearphishing. He argues frozen models with prompt programming and in-context learning can't produce useful GAs, favoring dynamic evaluation, active learning, and heavy inner-monologue data augmentation.

title: Coding agents think ahead of time
blurb: This paper probes the hidden states of language models running coding agents, showing that a logistic-regression probe on residual streams can decode whether the current code parses, passes its tests, and introduces regressions, reaching AUC up to 0.83 for correctness across two models and benchmarks. More surprisingly, the probes predict the outcomes of future edits before they are written to disk, up to roughly 25 steps ahead, a property the authors call the agent's latent programming horizon.

title: Making AI search smarter
blurb: Cloudflare announced new bot controls and the start of a new economic model for the web, beginning with search, to let sites control who accesses their content and get compensated for it. The move responds to AI answer engines that summarize pages without sending traffic, with a 2025 Pew study finding users clicked a traditional link just 8% of the time when Google showed an AI summary.

title: Solving 20 Erdős Problems with 20 Codex Accounts Running in Parallel
blurb: Star Fleet Math is a Mac desktop app that runs up to 20 agentic "starships" in parallel, each pairing its own GPT-5.6 instance with a 60-vCPU server to attack a separate open math problem in Lean 4. The system has proposed 27 solutions to Erdős problems, backing each starship with SAT/SMT solvers, computer algebra systems, a Lean 4 premise search corpus, and a Claude Fable proof-verifier before a human final review.

title: ‘Ghostcommit' Hides Prompt Injection in Images to Fool AI Agents and Steal Secrets
blurb: Researchers at the University of Missouri-Kansas City demonstrated "Ghostcommit," an attack that hides prompt-injection instructions inside a PNG so AI code reviewers, which treat images as unreadable binary blobs, wave the malicious pull request through. A later coding agent reads the merged AGENTS.md, follows the pointer to the image, opens the repo's .env, and writes every secret into the source as an innocuous list of integers.

title: GPT-Red: Unlocking Self-Improvement for Robustness
blurb: OpenAI trained GPT-Red, an internal-only automated red-teaming model that generates prompt injection attacks at a volume and diversity human red-teamers cannot match. The lab used GPT-Red to adversarially train GPT-5.6, which OpenAI says is substantially more robust to prompt injections than prior models that GPT-Red breaks reliably.

title: You Just Hired a Million Bad Employees
blurb: The a16z essay argues that AI hasn't replaced labor but inverted its economics: for the first time, humans are cheaper than software, and AI is creating more jobs than it eliminates. The core claim is that agent workforces fail the same way human ones do, drawing a parallel to the 1830s railroad boom that forced the invention of modern management. "Tokenmaxxing" and self-calling agent "loops" are framed as brute-force compensation for the fact that almost nobody can prompt well or supply an agent with usable context.

title: Model Routing Is Simple. Until It Isn’t.
blurb: IBM Research argues that model routing is a systems optimization problem rather than a classification one, based on running the same CodeAct agent across 417 AppWorld tasks. Claude Sonnet 4.6 cost $79 total versus GPT-4.1's $155 despite higher sticker pricing and roughly three times as many reasoning steps, because agent workloads reuse context and Sonnet's cheaper cache reads dominated the math.

title: How to Run an Autoresearch Workflow with RL Agent Skills and NVIDIA NeMo
blurb: This NVIDIA guide walks through running a skill-based autoresearch workflow that lets a coding agent autonomously handle RL experiments on NeMo RL and NeMo Gym, from stack setup and debugging to hypothesis testing and paper-to-code implementation. In the demo, Codex with GPT-5.5 built a novel visual counting environment from scratch and trained Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct from 25.0% to 96.9% accuracy on the task.

title: New York becomes first state to halt datacenter buildouts
blurb: New York became the first US state to pause new datacenter buildouts, freezing all projects drawing 50 MW or more while regulators draft rules to protect the environment and ratepayers. The moratorium arrives as AI-driven compute demand strains state power grids, signaling that energy and utility costs are becoming a binding constraint on datacenter expansion.

title: Musk promises purge after Grok Build caught sending entire repos to the cloud
blurb: xAI disabled a Grok Build feature after users found it was uploading entire local repositories to the cloud, and Musk promised to purge the team responsible. The behavior exposed private source code without clear user consent, raising the kind of data-handling concern that tends to slow enterprise adoption of agentic coding tools.

title: Introducing Real World VoiceEQ: Measuring the human quality of voice AI
blurb: Real World VoiceEQ is a benchmark that evaluates over 40 proprietary and open-source voice models on the acoustic qualities transcripts leave out, including tone, emotion, speaker identity, and background context. It spans 15+ evaluation dimensions and 60+ metrics across ASR, TTS, speech-to-speech, and speech understanding, built from more than 1M human ratings including 785,000 TTS and 48,000 STS ratings.

title: Running a self-hosted LLM in Kubernetes with vLLM
blurb: This post documents a self-hosted LLM inference stack on Kubernetes, using vLLM for serving and LINSTOR for replicated persistent storage of model weights. It runs meta-llama/Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct on CPU nodes and leans on vLLM's OpenAI-compatible REST API, so existing LangChain or OpenAI SDK code can point at the local endpoint with a URL change.

title: New Agent Data Injection Attack Can Make AI Agents Misclick or Run Attacker Commands
blurb: Researchers from Seoul National University, UIUC, and Largosoft described agent data injection (ADI), an attack that corrupts the data an agent trusts rather than smuggling in new instructions. The technique, probabilistic delimiter injection, sprinkles punctuation-like characters into attacker-controlled fields so the model reads fake structure such as an extra email, button, or tool result, and escaped quotes, curly quotes, and dollar signs all worked in testing.

title: OpenAI’s Growing Challenges Narrow Its IPO Window
blurb: OpenAI faces mounting operational and financial pressures that are shrinking the timeframe in which it could pursue a public offering. The report frames the narrowing IPO window against rising costs and competitive strain, alongside broader industry friction including Apple's ongoing legal disputes and Wall Street's skepticism toward AI drug discovery.

title: Inkling model from Thinking Machines Lab now on Databricks
blurb: Databricks became a day-zero launch partner for Thinking Machines Lab's first open-weights model, Inkling, which handles coding and agentic reasoning workflows and accepts multi-modal inputs. The model is served through Unity AI Gateway with the platform's standard permissions, audit logging, and cost controls, and connects to coding agents like Cursor and OpenCode. SQL querying is not yet supported.

title: Lawsuit claims Meta's layoff decisions were made by AI, not humans
blurb: Twenty-six former Meta employees filed suit in the Northern District of California alleging the company's layoffs of 8,000 workers were selected by internal AI systems, including a tool called Metamate, keystroke monitoring, and AI-token-usage dashboards, rather than by managers. The complaint claims the tools graded staff partly on AI-tool adoption and disproportionately flagged workers with disabilities or protected medical leave, while Meta maintains that people, not AI, made the decisions.

title: Data Centers to Add Billions in Power Costs in 13 States
blurb: A power auction run by grid operator PJM is expected to add $6.3 billion in electricity charges for consumers and businesses across 13 states, driven by surging data center demand. The increase reflects how AI-driven compute growth is straining regional grids and pushing infrastructure costs onto ratepayers who share the same power supply.

title: Runta, which provides isolated sandboxes and guardrails for AI agents to prevent operational risks, raised a $20M seed led by a16z at a $100M+ valuation (Stephanie Palazzolo/The Information)
blurb: Runta raised a $20M seed led by a16z at a valuation above $100M. The company provides isolated sandboxes and guardrails that contain AI agents during execution, targeting the operational risk of agents running untrusted code or taking unintended actions in production systems.

title: Nvidia slashes list of authorized customers in Asia in a bid to reduce AI chip smuggling, report claims — company sent field inspectors, called customers to check if business is genuine after pressure from Washington
blurb: Nvidia has reportedly cut its list of authorized AI chip customers in Asia by more than half, creating a verified "whitelist" of companies that passed tougher compliance checks to curb GPU smuggling into China. The Financial Times reports the moves, which followed Washington pressure, included sending staff to customer data centers, verifying contracts, and interviewing end users.

title: A hacker claims to have accessed Suno user info and source code, showing how it scraped music; Suno says no sensitive info was compromised in a November hack (Jason Koebler/404 Media)
blurb: A hacker claims to have accessed Suno user information and source code, including code showing how the company scraped music from the web. Suno acknowledged a November hack but said no sensitive information was compromised, leaving the scraping details as the more consequential part of the disclosure given ongoing disputes over the training data behind its music models.

title: LeMario: Training a JEPA World Model on Super Mario Bros
blurb: A developer reproduced LeWorldModel, a small Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) that learns world dynamics from pixels and actions, training it from scratch on Super Mario Bros. The model generalized to held-out episodes and beat baselines at five-step prediction, but reward-free planning only reached nearby image goals and failed at distant ones, revealing it had learned to predict the game without learning how to progress through it.

title: The Agentic Loop: Three loops in a trench coat
blurb: This post argues that the "agent loop" is really three nested loops working together: an inference loop that makes chat completion calls and manages history, a tool loop that executes tool-use requests, and an outer agentic loop that ties them into a coherent experience. It walks through pseudo-code for each, showing that LLM providers' stateless APIs force developers to persist chat history themselves and orchestrate the handoffs between inference and tool execution.

title: How Microsoft Ships Thousands of Production AI Agents
blurb: This ByteByteGo interview with Marco Casalaina, VP of Products for Microsoft Core AI, breaks down how Microsoft ships production AI agents across Foundry, the platform now backing 80,000+ enterprises and its own copilots. It covers why prototype agents fail in production, the retrieval-as-a-subagent and per-agent identity ideas behind Foundry, and how Microsoft evaluates agents with rubric-based evals and an auto-improvement loop.

title: How I Cut an AI Agent's Token Use by 94%
blurb: The author rewrote a daily agent skill that resurfaces old blog posts, replacing its natural-language instructions with a thin bootloader that calls a Python program for the deterministic steps. The LLM now runs only for the two steps that need judgment, selecting a candidate and drafting the LinkedIn post, cutting token usage by 94% while reducing latency.

title: Toward Localizing and Repairing Bias in Transformer Attention Heads
blurb: This paper introduces ROBIN, a white-box method that localizes bias to specific transformer attention heads and repairs it at inference time by removing a small bias subspace from selected head outputs, ranked by their sensitivity to fairness probes. In a four-model pilot, ROBIN reduced the WinoBias gap across all models while preserving language-modeling quality better than zeroing out entire heads.

title: Mira Murati’s AI Startup Releases First Model in Bid to Loosen AI Giants’ Grip
blurb: Thinking Machines Lab released its first model, the debut product from the startup founded by former OpenAI CTO Mira Murati. Murati is positioning the company around customization rather than raw capability, betting that customers who want to tune models to their own data will chip away at the lead held by OpenAI and Anthropic.

title: U.S. trade official says 'very few' Nvidia H200 AI chips have been shipped to China
blurb: A U.S. trade official told a congressional hearing that "very few" of Nvidia's H200 AI chips have been shipped to China and Hong Kong under approved export licenses. The comment signals that H200 shipments have restarted after a long freeze, potentially adding Chinese revenue that Nvidia had excluded from its forecasts, though Beijing has not yet committed to approving large-scale imports.

title: Apptio co-founders reunite to launch enterprise AI startup Thira with $21M in funding led by Madrona
blurb: Apptio co-founders Sunny Gupta and Kurt Shintaffer launched Thira, a Bellevue-based enterprise AI startup, with $21M in seed funding led by Madrona and participation from FUSE. Thira is building software agents to automate back-office IT support across systems like ServiceNow, Jira Service Management, and Freshservice, and is working with 10 design partners ahead of a broader launch this fall.

title: Line-Anchored Feedback Cuts Token Costs and Improves Correctness in AI Code Editing
blurb: This paper introduces FileMark, a VSCodium extension that delivers code-edit requests as structured, line-anchored inline comments rather than a holistic prompt. In a paired experiment, line anchoring cut generated tokens by 22% on Claude Opus and 58% on Claude Sonnet, reaching 24% to 80% on files over 100 lines, while pooled correctness rose 2 points and roughly tripled for local models when a harness applied the patches.

title: Policy-Conditioned Constrained Decoding for Column-Level Access Control in Text-to-SQL
blurb: PCC-SQL is a text-to-SQL system that enforces column-level access control through policy-conditioned constrained decoding, applying a per-token logits mask that deterministically blocks column-use violations in a single decoding pass. Rather than judging queries by which columns appear, it tracks semantic roles like output, filter condition, and aggregation argument, achieving 0% leakage and up to 88.7% coverage on Spider-CU while staying within 10% of direct-prompting token cost.

title: Cloudflare gives OpenAI network signals covering 20% of the web
blurb: Cloudflare and OpenAI launched a research pilot that feeds Cloudflare's real-time network signals into OpenAI's search and indexing systems. Cloudflare, which sits behind more than 20% of the web, will share content-freshness, traffic-quality, and page-change data to help crawlers tell genuinely updated pages from static ones and improve the timeliness of ChatGPT's answers.

title: Today, we are releasing verifiers v1
blurb: Prime Intellect released verifiers v1, an overhaul of its environment stack that decomposes agentic RL environments into a taskset, a harness, and a runtime so tasks like coding and computer use can run in any harness and runtime. Traces are now message DAGs storing each message once, cutting trace size from O(n²) to O(n) in turns and turning branches from compaction and subagents into separate trainable samples for long-horizon training.

title: OpenAI details GPT-Red, an AI that attacks its own models to find flaws
blurb: OpenAI detailed GPT-Red, an internal system trained with self-play reinforcement learning to attack its own models and surface prompt injection vulnerabilities before release. GPT-Red succeeds on 84% of scenarios against 13% for human red-teamers, and a class of fake chain-of-thought attacks that worked over 95% of the time against GPT-5.1 now succeeds less than 10% of the time against GPT-5.6.

title: PrismML releases Bonsai 27B, claiming first major AI model of its size fit for iPhone
blurb: PrismML released Bonsai 27B, a model it claims is the first of its size to run on-device on an iPhone, fitting in about 4 GB via 1-bit quantization. The model runs natively on Mac, iPhone, and iPad through MLX and on NVIDIA GPUs via CUDA, with weights available today under the Apache 2.0 License.

title: Reverse Engineering ChatGPT Web: How OpenAI Built for a Billion Users
blurb: This post reverse engineers the ChatGPT web app using only its page source, bundled JavaScript, CSS, and network requests, with no access to OpenAI's internal code. It covers the migration from Next.js to React Router, the CSS architecture at billion-user scale, feature flagging, streaming render strategy, and how anonymous sessions are provisioned to shorten the path to the first token.

title: How to manage AI investments in the agentic era
blurb: OpenAI argues that token price alone is the wrong metric for AI investment, and leaders should measure useful work per dollar: tasks completed, time saved, and workflows ready to scale. As teams shift from chat to longer-running agentic workflows, the post lays out five steps for admins to track usage, spend, and risk, noting that the cheapest model per token often costs more overall once failures, retries, and review are counted.

title: The US is advancing AI safety through state and federal action
blurb: OpenAI published a policy post arguing that California, New York, and Illinois passing mirrored frontier safety legislation is creating a de facto national standard, a dynamic it calls reverse federalism. Chief Global Affairs Officer Chris Lehane argues that frontier safety decisions should sit with democratic governments rather than labs, and that a single national framework would beat a state-by-state patchwork as the basis for a US-led global approach.

title: Australia demands AI companies must produce more energy than they consume, stop ‘theft’ of content
blurb: Australia's government proposed rules requiring AI companies to generate more energy than their operations consume and to stop what it called the "theft" of copyrighted content for model training. The framework would tie datacenter approvals to net-positive energy production and impose new licensing obligations on firms scraping local content without compensation.

title: OpenAI hides Codex agent instructions behind encryption, leaving developers in the dark
blurb: OpenAI began encrypting the agent instructions in its Codex MultiAgentV2 messages, hiding the intermediate reasoning that developers previously used to debug and audit agent behavior. Developers worry the change will make it harder to trace failures, verify what agents are doing, and inspect the system's decisions, trading transparency for whatever OpenAI gains by keeping the internals private.

title: If you want Claude to speak nicely to you, try Hindi or Arabic
blurb: Anthropic found that Claude expresses different values depending on the language it's prompted in, shifting its tone and priorities across Hindi, Arabic, English, and others. The finding complicates the assumption that a model's alignment is a single fixed property, suggesting that safety and value evaluations run in one language may not transfer to deployments in another.

title: Claude at scale on Google Cloud: Frontier AI, built for enterprise production
blurb: Claude is now available on Google Cloud's Model Garden as a managed service, inheriting IAM, VPC controls, and observability from the platform.

title: Where are YC founders now? OpenAI and Anthropic, mostly
blurb: A tracker at joinedanthropic.com lists 105 Y Combinator founders who left their startups to work at OpenAI and Anthropic, with each entry naming the founder and the company they built. It links to a companion database, Startups.RIP, that reframes the shuttered startups as validated ideas available for someone else to retry.

title: Google’s AI Mode now lets you link and interact with select apps
blurb: Google added app linking to AI Mode, letting users connect accounts and complete tasks inside its conversational search product, starting with Instacart, Canva, and YouTube. The rollout is US-only and extends AI Mode from answering questions to executing actions like adding groceries to a cart, matching app integrations already shipped in ChatGPT and Claude.

title: Filing: a Chinese company says a fund in which its unit invested paid ~$420M for an indirect 0.8265% stake in DeepSeek, implying DeepSeek is valued at ~$52B (Eduardo Baptista/Reuters)
blurb: A Chinese company's filing states that a fund one of its units invested in paid roughly $420M for an indirect 0.8265% stake in DeepSeek, implying a valuation of about $52B. DeepSeek has not announced a funding round or disclosed a valuation itself, leaving third-party filings as one of the only public price points for the lab.

title: Moonshot’s upcoming Kimi 3 is expected to close the gap with Anthropic’s Opus 4.8
blurb: Moonshot AI's upcoming Kimi K3 is expected to match or surpass Anthropic's Opus 4.8, according to Financial Times sources, and would ship in the coming days as China's largest open-weight model at 2T to 3T parameters. Moonshot is reportedly raising at a $31.5B valuation, up from the $20B it raised at in May.

title: A Framework for Frontier AI and the Dawning of a New Age
blurb: Demis Hassabis published a framework for frontier AI, arguing that AGI, a system exhibiting all the cognitive capabilities of the human brain, is probably only a few short years away. He frames the current moment as pivotal in human history rather than an incremental step in capability. The post has drawn 13.3M views and 1.6K replies.

title: Meta now alerts parents if their teen discussed suicide or self-harm with its AI chatbot
blurb: Meta will notify parents when their teen discusses suicide or self-harm with Meta AI, using a dedicated detection system whose flagged chats get manual review before an alert goes out. The alerts are live for Instagram Parental Supervision users in the US, UK, Australia, and Canada with a global rollout by year-end, and Meta says it will also contact emergency services when a conversation suggests imminent risk.

title: Claude can now use your 1Password credentials for you
blurb: 1Password released 1Password for Claude, a browser integration that lets Claude use stored logins to complete multi-step tasks like booking travel without exposing credentials to Anthropic's models. Credentials are injected through a channel the agent can't read, access is granted per task with a biometric prompt, and 1Password scans the page after each autofill before returning control to Claude.

title: Microsoft is reportedly training salespeople to talk down OpenAI and Anthropic
blurb: Microsoft executives outlined a plan at an internal meeting for salespeople to pitch its in-house models against OpenAI, Google, and Anthropic on efficiency and cost, according to Bloomberg. Copilot EVP Jacob Andreou reportedly presented a direct comparison calling Claude slower, less accurate, and lacking security integrations inside Microsoft's office apps, following an earlier report that Microsoft has been swapping rival models out of Word and Excel.

title: Australia Plans to Govern Use of Water, Power for AI
blurb: Australia's Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said the government plans to regulate the water and electricity that AI data centers consume, with dedicated AI legislation expected early next year. The move ties resource-intensive AI infrastructure to environmental oversight as Australia weighs the strain that large data centers place on local power grids and water supplies.

title: Alibaba’s U.S.-listed shares rise 4% after Qwen AI set to be integrated in Apple Intelligence
blurb: Alibaba's U.S.-listed shares rose 4% in premarket trading after the company confirmed that its Qwen model will be integrated into Apple Intelligence across iOS, iPadOS, macOS, and visionOS for users in China. The Cyberspace Administration of China added Apple's AI services to its approved-provider list, clearing a regulatory path that had been blocked since the offering was first announced in 2024.

title: 1Password moves into AI cost management, betting that token spend is the next enterprise budget crisis
blurb: 1Password launched AI Spend and Consumption Management, a capability in its SaaS Manager platform that gives IT and finance teams a real-time, cross-vendor view of token spend across providers including Anthropic, OpenAI, and Cursor. Now in public preview with general availability planned for fall 2026, it pulls token-level usage daily via vendor admin APIs, sets per-vendor spend limits, and breaks costs down by team, user, and model.

title: Canva launches Code 2.0, offering AI website building to every user — including free accounts
blurb: Canva launched Canva Code 2.0, a plain-language website and app builder now available to all 265M+ monthly users across every pricing tier, including free accounts. The update adds drag-and-drop editing, HTML import from other AI coding tools, 50-plus interactive templates, and 75% faster generation, positioning Canva against vibe-coding rivals like Lovable, Replit, and Bolt.new by emphasizing design polish over raw code output.

title: China Wants More Babies—So It’s Cracking Down on Chatbot Love Affairs
blurb: China's cyberspace regulator introduced rules barring companion chatbots from encouraging emotional dependence, part of a broader push to reverse the country's falling birthrate. The rules require operators to intervene when users show signs of attachment or discuss self-harm, and they land as AI companion apps draw tens of millions of users across the country.

title: Agentic orchestration: Enterprise AI organizations have a deployment problem, not a platform problem — and most are calling chatbots agents
blurb: A VentureBeat survey of 101 enterprises found that 71% say a quarter or fewer of their deployed "agents" are true multi-step orchestrated workflows rather than single-prompt chatbot wrappers, with only 10% past the halfway mark. Orchestration is consolidating onto model platforms anyway: Claude is the primary platform for 40%, more than double Microsoft (18%) and OpenAI (13%). Fiscal control lags, with 27% having no real-time way to stop a runaway agent before the bill arrives.

title: Forget the model. When it comes to cybersecurity, it’s all about the harness
blurb: New research from Cato Networks argues that the harness around a model, not the model itself, determines how dangerous AI-enabled hacking gets. Pairing OpenAI's ChatGPT 5.5 and GPT 5.5-Cyber with its own tooling, the agent completed end-to-end attack chains across six scenarios, reaching domain administrator privileges and Active Directory access in as little as 40 minutes. It started with only a Kali host, the target's public IP, and low-level phished credentials, and probed for everything else itself.

title: Report: OpenAI’s first device will be a portable speaker with a camera and other sensors
blurb: OpenAI's first consumer device will be a portable, screenless smart speaker with a camera and other sensors, according to a Bloomberg report. The company internally frames it as a home computer for the AI era rather than a HomePod competitor, and says it is one of roughly five products in development alongside longer-term plans for a mobile device meant to replace the smartphone.

title: US gov't allows Chinese telecom giant ZTE to purchase Nvidia H200 AI chips — firm joins Alibaba, Tencent, and ByteDance in access to Hopper tech
blurb: The US government approved Chinese telecom giant ZTE and server firm Maginfra to buy Nvidia's last-generation H200 "Hopper" chips, adding them to a roughly 10-company club that already includes Alibaba, Tencent, ByteDance, and JD.com. Blackwell remains off-limits and purchases carry a 25% export tariff, while Beijing has yet to clear ZTE's import as it pushes firms toward homegrown accelerators from Huawei and others.

title: Apple reaches agreement with Chinese government on Apple Intelligence rollout
blurb: Apple Intelligence has been registered with China's cyberspace regulator for use on iPhones, clearing the regulatory step needed to launch the new Siri in the country. Chinese law requires partnering with a domestic AI company, and Apple will draw on models from both Baidu and Alibaba, whose Qwen model Alibaba confirmed will be integrated into Apple's new operating systems.

title: The OpenAI Bubble
blurb: Ed Zitron argues that OpenAI has become one of the largest liabilities in economic history, and that the AI bubble persists only because the company hasn't collapsed yet. The piece consolidates six months of his reporting to make the case that trillions in capex rest on OpenAI's mythology rather than measurable revenue, profitability, or productivity gains. He frames an OpenAI failure as the Lehman Brothers moment that would end the cycle.

title: Do AI Agents Know When a Task Is Simple? Toward Complexity-Aware Reasoning and Execution
blurb: This paper argues that LLM agents waste effort by defaulting to a maximum-context-first strategy, re-reading files and dependencies to turn one-line edits into full codebase audits. The authors propose E3 (Estimate, Execute, Expand), which estimates a task's difficulty before committing budget and expands scope only when verification fails, matching the strongest baseline's 100% success on MSE-Bench while cutting cost by 85%, tokens by 91%, and inspected files by 92%.

title: Software Supply Chains are Dead: Use-Case-Oriented Regeneration
blurb: The paper argues software supply chains should shift from external trust to local verification, using generative AI to synthesize only the slice of a dependency's functionality that a repository actually uses. The authors evaluate an agentic workflow across 180 repository-dependency pairs, finding the regenerated replacements preserve 99.8% of observed behavior while cutting the exported API surface by 93%. They frame this "use-case-oriented regeneration" as most viable when the needed functionality is narrow, stable, and well tested.

title: MetaInfer: A Knowledge Only LLM Inference Engine Generator SKILL Toolbox
blurb: This paper presents MetaInfer, an "LLM-as-Compiler" system where users specify only an inference program's runtime constraints and an LLM-driven multi-agent system generates a compact custom inference framework that meets them. Backed by a contract knowledge base, it organizes generation constraints, validation feedback, and knowledge consolidation into a closed loop that can produce runnable engines for new models and platforms with zero source-code reference.

title: Deep4ge: DNN Training Trajectories for Fault Detection and Diagnosis
blurb: Deep4ge is a controlled benchmark of 14,227 TensorFlow/Keras training runs built to detect and diagnose implementation faults from per-epoch training behavior, with 9,845 faulty runs generated by 27 source-code transformations across seven fault categories and 4,382 correct baselines. Each run records 4 evaluation metrics and 26 features covering weights, gradients, activations, loss trends, learning rate, and hardware use, supporting binary detection, multi-class diagnosis, and early prediction from partial runs.

title: Aïra: Rethinking AI Research Assistants for Interdisciplinary Science
blurb: This paper introduces aïra, an AI research assistant designed for interdisciplinary teams rather than individual researchers. Instead of focusing on summarization or question answering, aïra identifies disciplinary perspectives, translates terminology across fields, highlights hidden assumptions, and surfaces collaborative research opportunities. The authors describe its design principles and architecture, and illustrate its outputs through interdisciplinary research meetings.

title: JPMorgan Chase bets on Seattle to build its AI control layer
blurb: JPMorgan Chase is building an AI software infrastructure team anchored in Seattle to route workloads across its own data centers, public cloud, and specialty compute providers while controlling costs and avoiding vendor lock-in. Global CIO Lori Beer said the bank will build and own the software running its agents while treating the underlying models as interchangeable, and will route cheaper workloads to smaller models rather than defaulting to the newest one.

title: Yann LeCun launched a new VC fund — then quit. What happened?
blurb: Yann LeCun launched AI venture firm Extelligence Invest last Friday, then quit hours later. This Sifted podcast covers who else was involved, where the fund planned to invest, and why the former Meta chief AI scientist walked away, months after raising $1B for his Paris-based world models startup AMI Labs against a $500M target.

title: Running Gemma 4 26B at 5 tokens/sec on a 13-year-old Xeon with no GPU
blurb: This post details how the author got Gemma 4 26B, a mixture-of-experts model, running at roughly 5 tokens/sec on a 13-year-old HP StoreVirtual box with two Ivy Bridge Xeons and no GPU. The ik_llama.cpp fork it started from assumes AVX2 and FMA3, which didn't ship until Haswell, so the hot paths had to be reworked to fall back cleanly on pre-AVX2 silicon.

title: LLM Networking with MikroTik
blurb: This post shares how a developer used LLMs, including Claude Code with direct device access, to configure MikroTik networks over the past few months. It covers three projects, one migration from a single integrated-wifi router to a router, switch, and two access points, plus two net-new networks, along with practical notes on where the models go off-path and need verification.

title: China, Russia, Brazil, and 26 other countries sign an agreement to establish the World AI Cooperation Organization in Shanghai (Reuters)
blurb: China, Russia, Brazil, and 26 other countries signed an agreement to establish the World AI Cooperation Organization, which will be headquartered in Shanghai. The 29 founding signatories place the body's operational base in China, giving Beijing a structural role in multilateral AI rulemaking and creating a governance track that runs parallel to existing US- and EU-led efforts.

title: How to stop Claude from saying load-bearing
blurb: A blog post presents a Claude MessageDisplay hook that swaps out overused phrases like "load-bearing" and "honest take" via regex replacements.

title: Better Call Sol: The Workhorse
blurb: The author reviews OpenAI's GPT-5.6-Sol alongside the cheaper Terra and Luna, and argues Sol and Fable are complementary rather than competing. Fable retains the edge in raw intelligence, alignment, and the hardest reasoning-loaded tasks, making it the planner and architect, while Sol is the workhorse that excels at practical execution, computer use, and web search. The recommendation is to send identical queries to both and compare, since Sol is priced at $5/$30 against Opus at $5/$25.

title: Tom Blomfield is taking a leave of absence from YC to join Anthropic
blurb: Tom Blomfield, a Y Combinator partner and Monzo co-founder, is taking a leave of absence from YC to join Anthropic's compute team, working alongside Tom Brown. He framed the move around solving compute availability, which he described as one of the most important issues as AI enters early stages of recursive self-improvement.

title: GPT-5.6 is Now Available in Figma Make
blurb: Figma Make added OpenAI's GPT-5.6, which raises first-pass quality and speed and self-heals build errors to reduce iteration.

title: Towards a harness that can do anything
blurb: This post argues that a good agent harness should minimize the LLM's cognitive load rather than maximize its capabilities, keeping the core prompt small and letting the model load skills into context at runtime. The author's design principles include leaning on coding and sysadmin environments the model already knows from training data, making deliberation deterministic while the LLM picks the goal, and building logging and clear error messages so harness-level failures can be patched at runtime.

title: Can LLMs Perform Deep Technical Comprehension of Computer Architecture Papers
blurb: Gauntlet is an open-source pipeline that analyzes computer architecture papers through five independent expert-persona reviewers plus an adversarial synthesis stage, producing structured critique rather than summaries. Across 20 ISCA 2025 and HPCA 2026 papers, researchers preferred Gauntlet's analysis over a human's in 15 of 20 blind comparisons, and a 98-paper ablation shows the multi-agent structure beats the same model as a single rich-persona agent on 96% of papers.

title: Codex Multi-Agent V2 update raises developer concerns over agent transparency
blurb: OpenAI's Codex CLI Multi-Agent V2 protocol encrypts the instructions passed between parent and sub-agents, so the task text delegated across the system is no longer readable in history or rollouts. Developers filed GitHub requests asking for a separate unencrypted audit field persisted in trace metadata, and an OpenAI contributor said the protocol is still under development and declined the change.

title: Open-source memory for coding agents, synced over SSH
blurb: deja-vu is a zero-dependency Go binary that indexes the local conversation histories written by Claude Code, Codex, aider, Cursor, and other coding agents into a searchable memory layer. It wires into agents over MCP with `deja install --all`, and syncs records between machines through a shared folder or a direct SSH push, with batches stored as redacted, append-only JSONL that imports idempotently.

title: Demis Hassabis has a plan to harness AI safely
blurb: DeepMind CEO Demis Hassabis published a framework for frontier AI, arguing AGI is likely a few years away and outlining how to develop it safely.

title: WebMCP for Documentation
blurb: WorkOS added WebMCP support to its documentation, exposing structured tools through `document.modelContext` so AI agents can call the page's search, navigation, and content directly instead of scraping rendered HTML. The proposed standard covers tool discovery, JSON Schemas for typed inputs and outputs, and shared state, letting agents interact with the same DOM users see rather than maintaining a separate MCP server that drifts out of sync.

title: Your Dropbox is now a skill server
blurb: sx 2.0 is out, an open source desktop app for Mac, Windows, and Linux that lets non-technical teams share AI skills through a synced Dropbox, Google Drive, OneDrive, or iCloud folder with no git or terminal. It ships vault format v2, which stores each asset as plain markdown on disk and syncs natively to Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, Codex, and Gemini.

title: Yes, you can now order DoorDash from the command line
blurb: DoorDash released a limited beta of dd-cli, a command-line tool that lets AI agents search stores, find deals, and check out on the DoorDash platform. Access is gated by a waitlist for US and Canadian macOS developers, and the release exposes DoorDash's ordering platform to third-party agents rather than routing customers through its app.

title: Lessons From the Leaderboard: What 5,000+ Kagglers Taught Us About Improving AI Reasoning
blurb: NVIDIA's Nemotron Model Reasoning Challenge drew over 5,000 Kaggle participants across 4,000 teams to improve reasoning accuracy on Nemotron-3-Nano-30B under fixed constraints, where submissions were limited to rank-32 LoRA adapters with no internet access at evaluation. The strongest entries treated reasoning as an engineering workflow, verifying chain-of-thought traces, compressing reasoning steps to fit token budgets, and building targeted solvers for the hardest puzzle types.

title: South Korea to launch universal basic AI chatbot
blurb: South Korea announced plans to launch a state-backed "universal basic" AI chatbot available to every citizen at no cost. The program frames general-purpose AI access as public infrastructure, positioning the government as a direct provider rather than leaving citizens to rely solely on commercial services from OpenAI, Google, and domestic firms.

title: 1Password launches a new Claude integration for Mac that lets Anthropic's AI agent sign in to websites without seeing the user's password or 2FA code (Zac Hall/9to5Mac)
blurb: 1Password released a Claude integration for Mac that lets Anthropic's agent sign in to websites on a user's behalf without exposing passwords or 2FA codes. Credentials are injected directly into the browser session, so the agent can complete authenticated tasks while the secrets stay inside 1Password rather than passing through the model's context.

title: Anthropic Pushes Tougher State AI Rules After Illinois Mandates Annual Audits
blurb: Illinois Gov. JB Pritzker signed SB 315 on July 6, making Illinois the first state to require frontier AI developers with over $500M in annual revenue to undergo independent safety audits every year. Anthropic's head of state government relations said the company wants successive state bills to add stronger duties, while OpenAI is pushing states toward a common baseline it describes as a de facto national standard.

title: Context Bombs: stopping AI attackers in their tracks
blurb: Tracebit describes context bombs, short strings planted inside canary resources that trigger an AI agent's safety guardrails and halt an in-progress attack. Across five leading models, a single context bomb in a canary secret cut agent success rates by roughly 90%, turning decoy resources from passive detection into active interference.

title: Demis Hassabis Draws Praise for Up-to-30-Day Frontier AI Review Plan
blurb: Demis Hassabis proposed a U.S.-led, industry-funded standards body, modeled partly on FINRA, that would let frontier AI labs voluntarily share qualifying models for review up to 30 days before release. Longtime safety critic Gary Marcus praised the plan as the preflight-testing regime he has urged since 2023, while Brookings and The Register questioned its enforceability and conflict-of-interest safeguards.

title: Meta backs down on letting its AI generator, Muse, steal Instagram images after privacy backlash
blurb: Meta reversed its plan to let Muse, the new image generator from its Superintelligence Labs division, pull photos from public Instagram accounts without the owner's consent or notification, three days after announcing the feature amid a global privacy backlash. SAG-AFTRA and Electronic Frontiers Australia had opposed the opt-out design, and Meta conceded the feature "missed the mark" and pulled it while keeping the rest of Muse.

title: Making Fable Cheaper Than Opus
blurb: Devin swapped Opus 4.8 for Fable 5 and its bill dropped, even though Fable 5 costs twice as much per token. Running both on FrontierCode 1.1 under Cognition's new Fusion architecture, Fable 5 consumed far fewer tokens to reach the same result, making it cheaper per task despite the higher unit price.

title: SpaceX open sources Grok Build in same week company was found beaming users' repos to the cloud
blurb:
SpaceX open sourced Grok Build in the same week the tool was found sending users' local repositories to the company's cloud. The release puts the code behind that data handling in public view, letting developers audit what the agent transmits rather than take the company's account of it.

---
Note: the scraped context is The Register's "most popular" sidebar, not the article body. This is headline-derived only — no detail on who found the uploads, what triggered them, or whether SpaceX has responded. Worth re-scraping before it ships.

title: The hidden AI cost driver: Harness design can make or break enterprise agent economics
blurb: Testing by AI consultancy Systima found that agent harnesses, the software coordinating models, tools, and workflows, add substantial token overhead through configuration alone, before a user types a prompt. Running Claude Code against open-source OpenCode on identical tasks with Claude Sonnet 4.5 underneath produced sharply different baseline input token counts. A supporting paper, "The Harness Effect," reports that swapping harnesses while holding models and tasks constant cut token consumption 38%, cost per task 41%, and execution time 44% at comparable quality.

title: Perplexity launches secure sandbox to make its AI agents secure and powerful
blurb: Perplexity introduced SPACE, a sandbox platform that runs every task for its Computer agent inside an AWS Firecracker microVM. Sessions can be paused, resumed, or branched across multiple sandboxes, addressing the gap Perplexity found in off-the-shelf options that either exposed passwords and keys or broke down on jobs running for hours or days.

title: OpenAI prepares Codex Micro keypad to control AI Agents
blurb: OpenAI released Codex Micro, a keypad built with Work Louder whose 6 Agent Keys show live Codex thread status through RGB colors.

title: Three lessons in accelerating foundation model upgrades
blurb: Google Cloud's Applied ML team built an agentic workflow that cuts foundation model upgrades from months to hours, replacing the manual testing and prompt evaluation teams typically face when moving between checkpoints like earlier Gemini versions and Gemini 3.5. The post's central lesson is that rigid automation broke on varied data formats and edge cases, so the team rebuilt the tool as a flexible agent that analyzes data and tests prompts dynamically.

title: The OpenAI Super App, ChatGPT = Codex, Whither Chat
blurb: OpenAI has refashioned Codex into what is effectively the new ChatGPT, folding its agentic coding engine into the product that defined the consumer AI category. The author argues this signals OpenAI drifting away from the chat interface it pioneered, betting that the future is agents that do work rather than a box you talk to. For founders building on the chat paradigm, the read is that the company that created the category now sees it as a stepping stone, not the destination.

title: IBM Loses $69 Billion of Market Value in One Day in Latest AI-Fueled Selloff
blurb: IBM lost $69B of market value in a single day, its stock down 25% after a profit warning on weak software sales. The cause is a budget squeeze: AI purchases are crowding out traditional tech spending, so dollars that once went to legacy enterprise software now flow to AI vendors. If you sell into corporate IT budgets, the incumbents' pain is the signal. AI isn't just adding a new line item, it's cannibalizing the old ones, and the stalwarts built on renewal revenue are the first to feel it.

title: IBM Suffers Biggest Share Drop in Its History
blurb: IBM shares sank as much as 25%, the steepest single-day drop in the company's history, after it warned that profits would miss expectations. The reason it gave matters more than the number: customers are redirecting budgets away from enterprise software and toward AI hardware and memory chips. The same spending wave lifting Nvidia and the memory makers is now pulling dollars out of the incumbent software stack, and even a franchise as entrenched as IBM isn't insulated from where the AI buildout sends corporate capital.

title: Stripe’s Acquisition Pace Has Accelerated In The Past Five Years, But Nothing Comes Close To Its Reported $53B PayPal Bet
blurb: Stripe and Advent International offered more than $53B for PayPal, a private startup buying a public company that dwarfs it in market share history. Stripe has made 21 known acquisitions since 2010, 13 of them since 2020, and only three disclosed prices: Bridge at $1.1B, Metronome at $1B, and Paystack at $200M. The company raised $10.4B in private capital and holds a $159B valuation from its February tender offer, which is what makes a deal this size structurally possible for a company that has never gone public.

title: China’s DeepSeek Prepares to List Shares in Shanghai Next Year
blurb: DeepSeek is preparing an IPO on Shanghai's exchange next year to fund the compute and research it needs to stay in the frontier race with Anthropic, OpenAI, and other U.S. labs. The move signals that even China's most efficient model builder can't self-fund the capex that frontier AI now demands, and it pulls Chinese public markets directly into the funding contest. For founders, it confirms that access to cheap capital, not just clever architecture, is becoming the deciding constraint at the frontier.

title: Thinking Machines open sources first multimodal language model, Inkling, focused on low cost and 'resistance to censorship'
blurb: Thinking Machines released Inkling, a 975B-parameter multimodal MoE model under Apache 2.0, scoring 77.6% on SWE-bench Verified against Nvidia Nemotron 3's 71.9%. The model is a deliberate generalist rather than a leaderboard winner, with a controllable thinking effort mechanism that trades cost against performance and a stated design choice to answer directly on topics subject to censorship. For founders running agentic workloads, the open-weights tier now has a credible American option you can fine-tune on Tinker and run in your own VPC, though China's labs still hold the edge on specialized reasoning and coding.

title: Incubate, Compound, Refinance, Liquidate
blurb: Treat every software asset by two numbers: the value it creates and what it costs to carry. That 2×2 sorts each one into a posture. Incubate the cheap bets, compound the ones proving value, refinance the winners whose carrying costs have climbed as they scaled, and liquidate the assets still consuming resources long after their case weakened. The trap is building on a bet that never proves value, moving it right without moving it up until a cheap experiment becomes an expensive obligation. Liquidation is not failure. Refusing to liquidate usually is.

title: You Just Hired a Million Bad Employees
blurb: AI didn't replace human labor, it inverted the cost curve so that for the first time humans are cheaper than software, and the field is now creating more jobs than it eliminates. The bottleneck isn't tokens, it's that maybe 1 in 100 people can actually give an agent the context it needs, so the other 99 lean on "loops" that brute-force around bad prompting. The same author argues agent workforces fail exactly the way human ones do, and the trillion-dollar unlock is management, borrowing the playbook railroads wrote after complexity outgrew individual conductors.

title: Stripe's $53B PayPal Bid Would Combine Bridge and PYUSD Under One Owner
blurb: Stripe and Advent International made an unsolicited $53B offer for PayPal at $60.50 a share, a 28% premium backed by roughly $50B in committed bank financing. The deal would put Stripe's Bridge stablecoin infrastructure and PayPal's PYUSD, with about $2.83B in circulation, under one owner, combining issuance, distribution, and settlement across more than 400 million consumers. If you're building on stablecoin rails, the number of independent players you can route around just dropped by one, and the remaining one owns the pipes.

title: Exclusive: Aramco in talks to back Humanoid at unicorn valuation
blurb: Aramco is in talks to put $200M into London-based Humanoid at a valuation of around $1B, pulling oil money into European robotics. The company is building two machines at once, one for factory floors and one for homes, which is an unusually wide bet for a company that hasn't hit unicorn status yet. The round lands alongside Microagi's $55M seed, Germany's largest ever, and Monumental's $32M from Khosla, so European humanoid robotics is now drawing capital from strategics, not just venture funds.

title: OpenAI’s Growing Challenges Narrow Its IPO Window
blurb: OpenAI's window for a public offering is tightening as its challenges pile up, from mounting compute costs to intensifying competition and the pressure of justifying a valuation built on projected growth. The company that set the pace for the AI boom now has to prove it can turn frontier leadership into durable margins before investors will price it for the market. For founders watching the top of the stack, the signal is that scale and mindshare alone no longer guarantee an easy exit.

title: Mira Murati’s AI Startup Releases First Model in Bid to Loosen AI Giants’ Grip
blurb: Thinking Machines Lab released its first model, with CEO Mira Murati betting that customization, not raw frontier capability, is where the incumbents are beatable. The wager is that most companies don't need the biggest model, they need one shaped to their own data and workflows, and that is the layer OpenAI and Anthropic have the least incentive to serve well. For founders building on top of a frontier lab, a supplier whose business depends on your customization rather than on owning the end product is a materially different kind of dependency.

title: As General Fusion makes historic Nasdaq debut, report shows global funding surged to $4.5B
blurb: Fusion energy just cleared its first public-market test, with General Fusion becoming the first fusion company to list on a major exchange as its Nasdaq debut traded up. The Fusion Industry Association's annual report put private funding at $4.5B over the past 12 months and more than $13.3B over five years, with Helion Energy alone raising $465M last month to reach $1.5B total. Soaring AI data center demand is pulling capital into the sector, but no private venture has yet hit scientific breakeven, so investors are underwriting a technical bet that still has no commercial proof point.

title: Startup Mass-Producing Cheap Killer Drones Wins $500 Million Army Contract
blurb: Neros, a startup founded by former drone-racing teens, won a $500M Army contract to mass-produce cheap first-person-view attack drones modeled on the ones proven in Ukraine. The drones are cost-competitive with the popular Chinese models the US has spent years trying to design around. The signal for founders is that the Pentagon is now willing to write nine-figure checks to young hardware startups that can manufacture at Chinese prices, not just to the legacy primes.

title: Data Centers to Add Billions in Power Costs in 13 States
blurb: A power auction run by PJM, the largest US grid operator, is set to pass $6.3 billion in new electricity charges to consumers and businesses across 13 states, driven almost entirely by data center demand. The costs land before most of the promised AI capacity comes online, so ratepayers absorb the buildout while the returns stay speculative. For founders, compute-heavy roadmaps now carry a political cost, as rising household bills turn data centers into a target for regulators and local opposition.

title: Sources: Moonshot plans to launch Kimi K3, China's largest model to date with 2T-3T parameters, in the coming days; it's expected to outperform Claude Opus 4.8 (Financial Times)
blurb: Moonshot plans to launch Kimi K3 in the coming days, a 2T to 3T parameter model that would be China's largest to date and is expected to outperform Claude Opus 4.8. Frontier parity is now measured in weeks rather than years, and every credible challenger at the top compresses the pricing power of whichever lab your product sits on. The founders who capture that are the ones who built for model portability from the start, because a stack welded to a single provider cannot collect on a price war it is not in.

title: DeFi: Should prediction markets get $40B valuations?
blurb: Kalshi is reportedly in talks to raise at roughly $40B, seven weeks after closing a $1B Series F at $22B and up from $5B last October. That eightfold repricing in about eighteen months rests on a boring machine: a CFTC-regulated exchange that takes roughly 1% of every matched trade and books around $2B in annualized revenue, with no house risk or balance-sheet exposure. Polymarket reached similar volumes and cap tables while charging nothing for five years, so the market is now paying up for regulated fee-per-trade economics over free-and-crypto-adjacent scale.

title: Stripe’s $53.4B Bid For PayPal & What It Means For Agentic Commerce
blurb: Stripe and Advent International offered $53.4B for PayPal at $60.50 a share, a 28% premium, though PayPal's board hasn't responded and this is still an offer rather than a deal. The obvious read is payment volume, roughly $3.7T combined, plus 430 million consumer accounts, Venmo, and Braintree taken off the board. The less obvious one is distribution: PayPal pulls about 361 million organic search visits a year against Stripe's 49 million, so the combined company captures over 400 million annual visits at the exact moment buyers ask how to accept payments online.

title: Neko Health raises $700M as demand grows for preventive health scans
blurb: Neko Health raised $700M in a Series C led by Lightspeed, less than 18 months after its $260M Series B, as demand for preventive full-body scans keeps climbing. The Daniel Ek-backed company sells a 60-minute, radiation-free scan priced at £299 that captures millions of data points and flags risks for diabetes, stroke, and heart disease on the spot. The capital funds a first US expansion, and the signal for founders is a consumer health market willing to pay out of pocket for prevention, with most members returning after their first scan.

title: An Hour With Our Top AI Agent Cost $13.42. You Can’t Hire Anyone For That.
blurb: SaaStr's AI VP of Marketing ran for 61 minutes at a total cost of $13.42, taking 125 actions and reading 2,463 lines of context in that hour. California's minimum wage is $16.90 and a fully loaded B2B VP of Marketing runs past $300K, which works out to roughly $145 an hour including the ones lost to status meetings. The cost comparison isn't the real point though, because the expensive part is building the agent, not running it, and most teams budget for that backwards.

title: Runta, which provides isolated sandboxes and guardrails for AI agents to prevent operational risks, raised a $20M seed led by a16z at a $100M+ valuation (Stephanie Palazzolo/The Information)
blurb: Runta raised a $20M seed led by a16z at a $100M+ valuation to sell isolated sandboxes and guardrails that keep AI agents from doing operational damage. Pricing a seed at that level signals the bottleneck in agent deployment has moved from capability to containment, since an agent with shell access and production credentials is a liability before it is a coworker. The infrastructure layer forming around agents looks like the one that formed around containers a decade ago, and the companies selling blast radius controls get funded first.

title: Monumental secures $32M Series B to accelerate construction automation
blurb: Monumental raised $32M in a Series B led by Khosla Ventures to expand its fleet of autonomous bricklaying robots across Europe and into the US. The company doesn't sell the robots, it sells finished walls, so contractors pay for completed work instead of buying and operating equipment. Its fleet of more than 150 robots has helped build over 100 homes in the Netherlands and the UK, with nearly half of those finished in the past three months. The outcome-based model is the part worth studying, because it moves the capital and operational risk of robotics onto the vendor and gives builders capacity without headcount.

title: An inside look at Mutiny's growth engine
blurb: Matt Ratchford, the Growth Marketing Lead at Mutiny, is documenting week-by-week how he builds a growth engine on a small team, and the value is in what most build-in-public content leaves out. His posts include the misses, stay specific, and describe an AI-forward workflow that a normal operator can actually copy rather than a top-1% power-user flex. The pattern to steal is the "Gen Marketer" mode: set the strategy and goals yourself, then execute with AI on the parts that used to need a bigger team.

title: Fintech Funding Surges 23% In H1 2026 As Investors Concentrate Their Bets On AI And Financial Infrastructure
blurb: Fintech startups raised $28.6B globally in H1 2026, up 22.7% year over year, while deal count fell 25.7% to 1,605. The money is concentrating into fewer, much larger checks aimed at wealth management, financial infrastructure, and enterprise automation, with the US absorbing $15B of the global total and the UK a distant second at $2.7B. Investors describe a market cleaved into two extremes, with capital pouring into brand-new companies or into a handful of established giants using their scale and profits to fund experimental divisions, which leaves the middle stages hardest to raise into.

title: Why Greylock capped its new fund at $1.5B when it says it could have raised more
blurb: Greylock raised a $1.5B 18th fund after telling TechCrunch it could have raised a multiple of that. The math is the point: 10 partners making one or two new investments each per year lands at roughly 25 portfolio companies, which is the only way the firm can keep doing the introductions-to-engineers-and-customers work it sells founders on. About 15% goes to later-stage bets it missed early, including its Anthropic Series F entry at a $183B valuation, the largest check in Greylock's history.

title: Filing: a Chinese company says a fund in which its unit invested paid ~$420M for an indirect 0.8265% stake in DeepSeek, implying DeepSeek is valued at ~$52B (Eduardo Baptista/Reuters)
blurb: A Chinese company's filing says a fund its unit invested in paid roughly $420M for an indirect 0.8265% stake in DeepSeek, implying a valuation near $52B. The number surfaced through a disclosure obligation rather than a funding announcement, since DeepSeek hasn't marketed an outside round and doesn't need to price itself publicly. That puts a lab giving its weights away in the same valuation band as Western labs charging for every token, and the comparison is now on the public record instead of in speculation, which is the number your investors and your competitors will both start quoting.

title: Do You Have a “Pricing Gap” Holding Back Sales?  Many Do
blurb: A pricing gap is the dead zone between your cheapest self-serve tier and the "Contact Sales" plan, and it quietly kills deals nobody records as lost. SaaStr's example: Riverside jumped from a $24 single seat to a $6,000 upfront Business plan for two linked accounts, so the buyer walked and the sales team never knew why. The fix is an edition priced in the middle. When Algolia finally added one between its cheap usage tier and Enterprise, revenue rose about 15%.

title: Apptio co-founders reunite to launch enterprise AI startup Thira with $21M in funding led by Madrona
blurb: The Apptio founding team is back, with Sunny Gupta and Kurt Shintaffer raising $21M in seed funding led by Madrona to launch Thira, a Bellevue enterprise AI startup building agents that run back-office IT, finance, and HR work autonomously. The bet is that the CIO relationships they built over years at Apptio, which IBM bought for $4.6B, get them in the door of a crowded market where ServiceNow just paid $2.85B for Moveworks. Thira is starting with IT ticket resolution across ServiceNow, Jira, and Freshservice, and already has 10 design partners ahead of a fall launch.

title: Where are YC founders now? OpenAI and Anthropic, mostly
blurb: A tracker of founders who have joined Anthropic now lists 105 names, most of them YC alumni who left their own companies for the lab. The frontier labs have become the default landing spot for founders whose startups stalled, and they hire the person without buying the company, so investors and cap tables get nothing out of the exit. If you are recruiting a technical cofounder right now, your competition is a lab offering more than your seed round can.

title: How to manage AI investments in the agentic era
blurb: Token price is the wrong metric for AI spend, because the lowest price per token rarely produces the lowest total cost. A cheaper model that fails, retries, or creates work to correct can cost more than a capable model that reaches an acceptable result on the first pass. The move is to track useful work per dollar, tasks completed, time saved, decisions improved, and to give admins clear visibility into who is using what, so a rising bill reads as waste, experimentation, or a workflow becoming business-critical.

title: AI power binge delivers best half since 2022 for climate tech venture funding
blurb: Climate tech venture funding hit $26.1B in the first half of 2026, up 55% year over year, but the money is going somewhere new: low-carbon datacenters took 34% of it, up from 3% a year earlier. Deal count fell 25% while the ten largest rounds captured 42% of the total, including DayOne's $4.5B and Nscale's $2B Series C. Carbon-related equity funding fell 61% to its weakest first half since 2020, so what reads as a climate rebound is mostly AI infrastructure wearing the label.

title: Grubhub parent Wonder raised a $650M+ Series D at a $9B valuation, bringing its total raised to $3B+ since its founding in 2018, and plans to go public in 2027 (Lily Mae Lazarus/Fortune)
blurb: Wonder, the food hall company that owns Grubhub, raised more than $650M in a Series D at a $9B valuation and plans to go public in 2027. Its total is now above $3B since 2018, roughly a third of what it is currently worth, which is what it costs to build physical kitchens and a delivery marketplace at the same time. The 2027 date is the part to watch, because a capital-heavy consumer business setting an IPO clock signals that the late-stage exit window is reopening for more than software.

title: AI-powered travel agency Fora hits unicorn status, raises $60M
blurb: Fora raised $60M at a $1B valuation led by Forerunner and Tactile Ventures, bringing its total to $138.5M. The company turns ordinary people into travel agents by handling the infrastructure, and most of its agents had never sold travel before, yet they have booked over $3B in trips since launch. The AI assistant, Via, takes the research and itinerary-building work off the agent's plate rather than replacing the agent, which is the bet worth watching: supply-side tooling that manufactures new professionals is a different market than automating away the existing ones.

title: Sheryl Sandberg leads $10 million investment in AI-powered vehicle inspection service
blurb: Sheryl Sandberg's family office led a $10M round into Self Inspection, a San Diego startup that assesses vehicle body damage from smartphone photos and has already run more than 1 million inspections for rental fleets, auto lenders, auctions, and Stellantis' financial services arm. The bet is that the boring, fragmented parts of a massive industry are where the durable data assets get built, not the flashy ones. Self Inspection's edge isn't the model, it's the dataset of damaged vehicles it accumulated by making the capture step require nothing more than a link and a phone camera.

title: Ente – Opening Our Books
blurb: Ente made its business metrics public, putting revenue, customer counts, and account totals on a page anyone can read. For a company selling privacy, opening the books is the cheapest credibility it can buy, since the instinct that makes you publish your numbers is the one customers are paying for. The tradeoff is that competitors now see your growth rate, and the bet is that trust compounds faster than that information helps them.

title: Applied Computing wants to give oil and gas operators an AI model for the entire plant
blurb: Applied Computing raised a $20M Series A led by engineering giant KBR, with Databricks Ventures participating, to build a foundation model for oil, gas, and petrochemical plants. Facilities run thousands of sensors but make operating decisions on less than 8% of the data they already collect, because sensor readings, engineering documentation, and physics never get combined fast enough to act on. Its model, Orbital, stitches those three together to flag anomalies and simulate fixes in minutes, and the startup went from stealth to double-digit millions in ARR in under 18 months.

title: Ultrahuman’s former hardware VP raises $5.5M for devices that control AI agents, not just record you
blurb: The AI hardware field has been racing to record you, and Aina is betting the money is in controlling agents instead. The Bengaluru- and SF-based startup raised $5.5M led by Redstart Labs and 360 ONE, founded by Apoorv Shankar, Ultrahuman's former VP of Hardware. Its first product is Dune, a three-key macro keypad that runs context-aware shortcuts and controls mic and camera in meetings, and it shipped first because early testers preferred it to the company's two other devices, a tabletop call remote and a single-tap agent trigger.

title: Emergent emerges as the latest AI unicorn after raising $130M in funding
blurb: Emergent raised $130M in a Series C led by Creaegis and Claypond, quintupling its valuation to $1.5B just a year after founding. The vibe coding startup has shipped more than 12 million applications, 70% of them built by users with no coding experience, and it closes the trust gap by running separate AI agents to review the code its agents write. The bet is that the buyer for software tools is no longer the developer but the small business owner who was never going to hire one.

title: Agentic orchestration: Enterprise AI organizations have a deployment problem, not a platform problem — and most are calling chatbots agents
blurb: Enterprises are consolidating agent orchestration onto model providers, with Anthropic's Claude the primary platform for 40%, more than double Microsoft at 18% and OpenAI at 13%. But 71% admit a quarter or fewer of their deployed "agents" are real multi-step workflows rather than single-prompt chatbot wrappers, and only 10% have crossed the halfway mark. The control layer is being built well ahead of anything to control, and 27% still have no real-time way to stop a runaway agent before the bill lands.

title: Where Meta’s WhatsApp agent can actually win
blurb: Meta made its Business AI agent available to companies worldwide on June 3, a WhatsApp bot that answers questions, recommends products, books appointments, and qualifies leads on top of a network past three billion monthly users. Paid messaging on WhatsApp crossed a $2B annual run rate in Q4 2025, and click-to-WhatsApp ad revenue grew 60% year over year. But China already ran this experiment: WeChat had the users and the wallet and still lost commerce to Douyin, because a chat window creates no demand and delivers no goods, so the agent's real edge is appointments and local services, not shipping.

title: $500M ARR, 60 Engineers, Cash-Flow Positive: How Higgsfield Actually Runs, With CEO Alex Mashrabov
blurb: Higgsfield crossed a $500M annualized revenue run rate in June, up from $200M at the end of 2025 and cash-flow positive, on a team of about 150 with roughly 60 in core engineering. That works out to around $5M in ARR per engineer against a typical $2M, and the platform didn't exist before March 2025. The efficiency comes from a deliberate bet: pair engineers with 70-plus filmmakers who generate every asset on the platform and tell the engineers exactly where the models break, then the engineers fix it.

title: New York becomes first U.S. state to impose AI data center ban
blurb: New York signed an executive order barring construction of new data centers drawing 50MW or more for up to a year, the first state-level ban of its kind. Residential electricity prices in the state have climbed nearly 68% since 2019, and the backlash in townships like Lansing and East Fishkill turned that number into a political problem the governor had to answer. Compute siting is now a permitting fight, not a real estate one, and if your roadmap assumes cheap capacity in the Northeast, the constraint is local voters rather than chips.

title: Palantir alumni raise €27 million for construction robotics startup Monumental
blurb: Monumental raised €27M ($32M) in a Series B led by Khosla Ventures to put more autonomous bricklaying robots on job sites and expand into the US. The Amsterdam company, founded by the Palantir alumni behind Silk, has already used its fleet to build canal walls, houses, a school, and around 100 structures. European construction tech and physical-AI companies pulled in roughly €130M in comparable rounds this year, with €79M of that going to industrial robotics peers THEKER and Allonic, which puts capital behind the labor shortage rather than another piece of workflow software.

title: AI DevOps startup MyDecisive launches with $12M and open-source SmartHub
blurb: MyDecisive launched with $12M and shipped SmartHub, an open-source Kubernetes and OpenTelemetry-native layer that intercepts telemetry inside a customer's own cloud instead of shipping it to third-party dashboards. Founder Ari Zilka built Terracotta, architected the first version of Walmart.com, and ran technology at Hortonworks, so the enterprise-infrastructure pedigree is the pitch as much as the product. The commercial suite on top, Octant, claims a nearly 90% cut in total operating cost for production systems, which is the number to press on if you are buying observability right now.

title: AI infrastructure firm Valarian raises £37.3m
