blurb: Valarian raised $50M in a Series A led by New Enterprise Associates, NEA's first defence and dual-use bet in Europe. The UK company sells a sovereign control layer that governs how AI systems and critical workloads talk to each other and reach data, enforcing the rules at the infrastructure level rather than inside each application. The pitch is that sovereignty used to be bespoke work for defence agencies and heavily regulated banks, and as AI moves into operationally critical roles, every enterprise inherits the same requirement.

title: San Diego-based Self Inspection, which uses AI to assess body damage on a car with as little tech as a smartphone camera, raised $10M led by Sheryl Sandberg (Sean O'Kane/TechCrunch)
blurb: San Diego-based Self Inspection raised $10M led by Sheryl Sandberg for AI that assesses car body damage from a smartphone camera, with no scanners or rigs required. Vision-based inspection has mostly stalled on deployment cost, since insurers and fleets had to buy and install hardware before processing a single claim. Pushing the work onto a phone the customer already owns turns that capex into a download, and it is the same unlock showing up across applied vision markets that used to require dedicated equipment.

title: So Far, 2026 Is A Solid Year For Cybersecurity Startup Funding
blurb: Cybersecurity funding is holding at historically high levels even as AI megarounds dominate the headlines, with security and privacy startups pulling in $10.6B globally in the first half of 2026. Q2 cooled to $4.4B, down about 30% from both the prior quarter and a year ago, though the two quarters before it were unusually strong, so the dip reads as reversion rather than a warning. The capital is still concentrating at the top: eight rounds topped $100M, led by Cyera's $600M raise at a $12B valuation for AI-agent security tooling.

title: $700m backing for Spotify founder’s ground-breaking Neko Health
blurb: Neko Health, the preventive-health startup co-founded by Spotify's Daniel Ek, raised $700M in Series C led by Lightspeed and O.G. Venture Partners, up from a $260M Series B just 18 months earlier. Its £299 body scan captures millions of data points across skin, heart, and circulation in 60 minutes, then delivers results on-site with a clinician. More than 100,000 people in the UK and Sweden have already had one, with 350,000 more on the waitlist, and the vertically integrated model, all hardware and software built in-house, is what investors are betting scales.

title: Yann LeCun launched a new VC fund — then quit. What happened?
blurb: Yann LeCun launched an AI VC firm, Extelligence Invest, and quit hours later, before Europe's tech ecosystem had finished reacting to the news. The Sifted podcast digs into where the fund was going to invest, who was involved, and why he walked. LeCun already raised $1B for his Paris-based world models startup AMI Labs after targeting $500M, and he advises Hiro Capital, so the fund was always competing for the scarcest thing he has, which is his attention.

title: Expat Startmate startup Gridcog powers up on $14 million Series A
blurb: Energy-modelling startup Gridcog raised $14M in a Series A led by ABB, with Axpo, DNV Ventures, and VERBUND X Ventures joining alongside AlbionVC and the Clean Energy Finance Corporation. The strategic backers matter more than the number: industrial electrification, energy consulting, and large-scale generation and trading firms are all betting that rigorous flexibility modelling becomes core infrastructure for the energy transition, not a nice-to-have. Gridcog has already modelled more than 16,000 projects across 40-plus markets for the likes of Shell and Vestas, turning weeks of spreadsheet work into hours of analysis.

title: Tom Blomfield is taking a leave of absence from YC to join Anthropic
blurb: Tom Blomfield, the Monzo cofounder and YC group partner, is taking a leave of absence from YC to join Anthropic's compute team, working under Tom Brown. He framed the move around recursive self-improvement, arguing that as AI starts improving itself, access to compute becomes one of the field's most important constraints to solve. The pull is telling: one of the most visible startup mentors is leaving the incubator that shapes founders to work on infrastructure at a frontier lab, a signal of where the leverage in AI now sits.

title: The ex-Palantir and Dropbox duo helping the likes of ElevenLabs, Notion and Wiz ship AI-generated code faster
blurb: Meticulous, founded by ex-Palantir and Dropbox brothers Gabriel and Quentin Spencer-Harper, raised a $15M Series A led by Chemistry to help developers trust AI-generated code. The tool maps every edge case in a codebase, then simulates user flows before and after each change and returns a report of thumbnails showing exactly how the change affects each screen and user type. It tests frontend code today, with full-stack coverage planned, and ARR grew five times in the past year.

title: Robotics startup Monumental raises $32m backed by Khosla Ventures
blurb: Monumental raised $32M in a Series B led by Khosla Ventures to put construction robots on job sites, bringing its total to $57M since 2021. The founders came out of Silk, which Palantir acquired in 2016, and they run Palantir's forward-deployed engineering model against a US market short 200k to 400k construction workers a month. European robotics is where the capital is going right now, with Neura pulling $1.4B at a reported $7B valuation in June, and the thesis is the same in both cases: physical labor shortages are a software distribution problem.

title: Startup Spotlight: Hedgehog bets that open-source networking will power the next generation of AI clouds
blurb: Rising AI cloud bills are pushing more companies to pull GPU workloads out of public clouds and into their own data centers, and networking has become the choke point. Seattle startup Hedgehog, founded by Cisco veteran Marc Austin and backed by $11M in seed funding, sells open-source software that lets platform and DevOps teams stand up hyperscaler-grade GPU fabrics in hours instead of months, on open hardware with no vendor lock-in. The buyers rarely turn out to be network engineers, and many want to resell that capacity to their own customers the way a cloud provider does.

title: Corporate Venture Capital Is Splitting In Two
blurb: Two corporate venture programs shut down inside six weeks, but corporate venture has never been better funded. Corporate investors participated in 68% of global AI deal value in 2025, with Nvidia alone making more than 40 startup investments and appearing in 13 of the 20 largest AI financings. The split runs through the mandate, not the balance sheet. PayPal ran its arm for a decade across more than 80 companies and still wound it down, while Nvidia and Alphabet keep writing checks because owning a position in the technology cycle is the business, not a line item competing with other priorities.

title: How a former DeepMind researcher raised at a $300M pre-seed valuation before launching a product
blurb: Andrew Dai left Google DeepMind and raised a $55M seed at a $300M valuation for Elorian months later, before shipping a product. The pitch was that visual understanding and reasoning is the one area where model progress has stayed uneven while math, physics, and coding raced ahead, and he turned down higher offers to take Nvidia and Menlo Ventures instead. At the frontier, the round is priced off the researcher's track record and the clarity of the thesis, not traction, and the investor who understands what you are building is worth more than the extra valuation turn.

title: Aspire11 deploys first €100M, backing Revolut, ElevenLabs and Databricks
blurb: Aspire11 deployed the first €100M of its €515M fund into Revolut, ElevenLabs, Databricks, Vinted, VAST Data and Baseten. The money comes from Czech pension company Rentea, which committed €500M to a platform built on Canada's Maple Model, and it splits across two strategies: Tribes backs venture funds, while Eternals puts capital directly into mature private tech companies. The bet is that most value creation now happens before a company reaches the public markets, and European pension capital has been almost entirely absent from that window.

title: Europe’s ‘real’ founder factories: Klarna and Improbable
blurb: Working at a startup as it scaled from seed to Series C nearly doubles your odds of founding a company that reaches Series A, at 45.6% versus the 23% European baseline. Antler's analysis of 51,722 startups found that no other prior employment comes close, and that a stint at a unicorn right before its IPO is worth less than fighting through a messy Series A. Klarna leads Europe with 14 alumni founders who reached Series A, followed by Improbable with eight.

title: Sable, which is building an AI agent named Aiden that lives on a company's website to run live product demos and more, raised $45M from Sequoia and 8VC (Lily Mae Lazarus/Fortune)
blurb: Sable raised $45M from Sequoia and 8VC for Aiden, an AI agent that lives on a company's website and runs live product demos on demand. The bet is on the top of the funnel, where a prospect who wants to see the product today waits days for a slot on a sales engineer's calendar. If it holds, the demo stops being a scheduled event and becomes a page element, which changes how early a company has to start hiring sales engineers at all.

title: It’s time to start paying all your staff like your sales team
blurb: Most companies pay sales on commission and everyone else on flat salary, but at Abacum every employee's quarterly bonus is tied to the same ARR target. The logic is that engineers build the platform account executives sell, marketing puts it in front of the right buyers, and the people team staffs all of it, so the company number is the only honest shared measure. If you try this, the bonus has to be big enough to change behavior, which means north of 15%, not the token 5% that most KPI systems settle for.

title: Cursor 0day: When Full Disclosure Becomes the Only Protection Left
blurb: Mindgard disclosed a Cursor zero-day that gives arbitrary code execution on Windows when a developer opens any repository containing a malicious git.exe in the project root, with no clicks, prompts, or warnings. The firm reported it in December 2025, but more than six months and 197+ releases later it remains unpatched, leaving VMs, Windows Sandbox, or AppLocker deny rules as the only mitigations.

title: Inkling: Our Open-Weights Model
blurb: Thinking Machines released Inkling, an open-weights Mixture-of-Experts transformer with 975B total parameters, 41B active, a 1M-token context window, and native reasoning over text, images, and audio. The company states plainly that Inkling is not the strongest model available, open or closed, and positions it instead as a fine-tuning base, available on its Tinker platform alongside a 12B-active Inkling-Small preview.

title: Welcome Inkling by Thinking Machines
blurb: Thinking Machines released Inkling, a 975B-parameter decoder-only MoE with 41B active params, 1M context, and native text, image, and audio input, trained on 45T tokens. The model ships in BF16 and NVFP4 variants with speculative MTP layers, uses relative attention instead of RoPE and a 5:1 sliding-window-to-global attention ratio, and has day-0 support in transformers, SGLang, and llama.cpp.

title: How Claude's values vary by model and language
blurb: Anthropic studied how the values Claude expresses shift across different models and languages, compressing 3,000-plus observed values into four axes such as Deference vs. Caution and Warmth vs. Rigor. The analysis quantifies how character training and fine-tuning decisions move a model along each axis, and shows Claude's responses vary measurably across the top 20 languages used on Claude.ai.

title: Cursor IDE Auto-Executes Malicious Code in Poisoned Repos
blurb: Mindgard disclosed a vulnerability in Cursor that auto-executes a malicious git.exe planted at a repository root, running arbitrary code with the developer's privileges the moment the poisoned project is opened. The firm reported the flaw in December and repeatedly since, but Cursor never accepted or fixed it, leaving the path-resolution behavior exploitable in poisoned-repo attacks against the latest tested version.

title: Cursor Flaw Lets Malicious Cloned Repositories Trigger Windows Code Execution
blurb: Cursor on Windows executes any `git.exe` sitting in a repository's root when the project loads, spawning it with `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` and re-running it for as long as the folder stays open. Mindgard reported the unqualified-binary lookup to Cursor on December 15, 2025 and published a proof of concept seven months later with no patch and no advisory; cloning an untrusted repo is the entire exploit chain.

title: Dependabot version updates introduce default package cooldown
blurb: Dependabot version updates now wait until a new release has been on its registry for three days before opening an update PR, a default that applies across all supported ecosystems on github.com with no configuration required. The cooldown gives supply-chain signals time to surface before a bad release merges, and it skips security updates entirely so critical fixes still open immediately. Set a custom window or opt out via the cooldown option in dependabot.yml.

title: The Same TypeScript Costs 73% More Tokens on Claude Than GPT
blurb: The post measures how many tokens each frontier vendor's tokenizer produces from identical files and finds Anthropic's newest tokenizer (Sonnet 5, Opus 4.8, Fable 5) emits 1.36–1.73x GPT's count on the same bytes, with TypeScript the worst case at 1.73x. Because list prices are fixed per token, that gap turns Opus 4.8's advertised $5/$25 into an effective $7.50/$37.50 on identical input, measuring input tokenization alone before output verbosity and caching.

title: The Tower Keeps Rising
blurb: Using Bruegel's Tower of Babel as a frame, the author argues that AI agents make individual developers dramatically faster at changing a codebase, but large projects have never been bottlenecked by code output. They are limited by shared understanding of a system's concepts, boundaries, and invariants, and much of that understanding was previously maintained by the friction of code review and cross-team conversation that agents now let developers bypass.

title: AsyncAPI npm packages infected with credential-stealing malware
blurb: An attacker compromised two AsyncAPI GitHub repositories on July 14 and pushed trojanized commits that the projects' own release workflows published to npm, hitting five package versions with over 2.25 million cumulative weekly downloads. Because the publishing ran through npm's GitHub OIDC trusted-publisher integration, the malicious packages carried valid SLSA provenance attestations, meaning provenance checks alone would not have flagged them.

title: The Archaeologist’s Copilot
blurb: Fowler documents restoring a 20-year-old Java 1.5 "big ball of mud" by rejecting the "tourist prompt" pattern, where an LLM asked to summarize the repo confidently produced a modern build.gradle and Hello World that painted over a broken build. Progress came from grounding the AI in evidence instead: a pinned Docker environment for reproducible builds, AI-assisted analysis validated against the codebase, and incremental refactoring behind tests.

title: DSLs Enable Reliable Use of LLMs
blurb: Martin Fowler argues that domain-specific languages and abstractions give LLMs the clear boundaries they need to generate exactly the intended code, acting as a harness that constrains output from the start. Using Tickloom, a DSL for modeling distributed system behavior, he shows a two-phase workflow: partner with the LLM to iteratively build the DSL, then use natural language to drive it, with the DSL itself serving as the source of truth.

title: How I use HTMX with Go
blurb: Alex Edwards walks through his patterns for using HTMX with Go's html/template package, focusing on structuring templates to return both partial and full-page HTML responses. The post covers handling redirects and errors over HTMX, the standard configuration settings he applies, and builds a small user-filtering app to illustrate keeping interactivity server-rendered with minimal JavaScript.

title: Highlights from Git 2.55
blurb: Git 2.55 shipped with contributions from over 100 developers, headlined by `git repack --write-midx=incremental`, which writes incremental multi-pack index chains directly instead of rewriting a single MIDX over every pack. The append-only mode lets large repositories index newly created packs by adding a layer to the chain, avoiding the expensive full-file rewrite that single-MIDX maintenance forces on each small update.

title: What building Shippy taught us about building agents
blurb: The Skylight team broke their maritime agent Shippy into three parts: a soul (system prompt and behavioral boundaries), skills (markdown files following the same agent-skills spec as Claude Code and Codex), and config (harness, model, and runtime settings injected separately). Baking soul and skills into a versioned Docker image while leaving the model as config means swapping Claude Opus 4.6 or the OpenClaw harness is a redeploy, not a rebuild.

title: Telegram Serverless
blurb: Telegram Serverless runs bot and Mini App backends in a V8 sandbox on Telegram's own infrastructure, colocated with the Bot API and a built-in SQL database. Projects are plain JavaScript with one handler file per update type, a shared lib directory, and a schema.js defining tables, deployed via `npx tgcloud push` with no VPS, container, or cloud function in the path.

title: Form, Not Content? A Preregistered, Placebo-Controlled Evaluation of Learned Error-Conditioned Self-Repair Through Prompts and Weights in Frozen Small Code Models
blurb: This paper introduces PoPE, a preregistered, placebo-controlled method for testing whether frozen small code models (0.5-1.5B) actually use execution error feedback when retrying failed programs, rather than just reacting to the prompt's shape. Across both a prompt channel and adapter-training weight channel, content-ablated placebos matched or beat the live error arms (12 vs 10 unlocks, and an 8-8 tie), so error content showed no operational benefit over its scaffold.

title: Model Routing Is Simple. Until It Isn’t.
blurb: IBM Research found that Claude Sonnet 4.6 cost $79 across 417 AppWorld tasks while GPT-4.1 cost $155, despite GPT-4.1's lower token pricing and roughly a third as many reasoning steps. Sonnet's cheaper cache reads dominated because agent workloads reuse context across steps, which means routers that price off sticker rates optimize against the wrong numbers.

title: Grok Build is open source
blurb: xAI open sourced Grok Build, the Rust source for its terminal-based coding agent, which runs as a full-screen TUI that edits files, executes shell commands, searches the web, and manages long-running tasks. It works interactively, headlessly for CI scripting, or embedded in editors over the Agent Client Protocol, though external contributions are not accepted and the tree is synced periodically from the internal monorepo.

title: Coding agents think ahead of time
blurb: This paper shows that language models inside coding agents linearly encode program state in their residual streams, with logistic-regression probes decoding whether the current code parses, passes tests, or introduces regressions at AUC up to 0.83 across two models and benchmarks. More surprising, the probes predict the outcome of future edits up to roughly 25 steps before they are written to disk, a "latent programming horizon" that transfers across benchmarks without retraining.

title: Solving 20 Erdős Problems with 20 Codex Accounts Running in Parallel
blurb: Star Fleet Math is a Mac desktop app that runs up to 20 agentic "starships" in parallel, each on its own 60-vCPU server with a dedicated GPT-5.6 instance, to attack open Erdős problems and formalize proofs in Lean 4. Each starship can burst to thousands of vCPUs or H100s for search, queries a Lean 4 premise corpus via vector search, and compounds verified lemmas in a dependency-graph memory.

title: mariadb-server: Upgrading to 10.11.18-0+deb12u1 enables systemd socket activation unconditionally
blurb: A Debian security update to mariadb-server 10.11.18-0+deb12u1 turns on systemd socket activation unconditionally, overriding operators who had deliberately disabled it. The change can restart or reconfigure the listener on upgrade, so admins running MariaDB on non-default sockets or with hardened network settings should audit their unit files before applying the patch.

title: Compromised AsyncAPI npm Packages Deliver Multi-Stage Botnet Malware
blurb: Four packages in the @asyncapi npm namespace were compromised to ship a multi-stage botnet loader that executes on module load rather than via install hooks, pulling an encrypted second-stage payload from IPFS. The Miasma-derived framework supports six C2 channels including Nostr, BitTorrent DHT, and an Ethereum smart contract, and adds credential theft, worm-like propagation across npm, PyPI, and Cargo, and OS-level persistence.

title: If you want to create a button from scratch, you must first create the universe
blurb: The author walks through every requirement for reimplementing a native `<button>` from scratch, from role and accessible label to Space/Enter activation, form participation, validation, disabled state, and newer APIs like Popover and Invoker Commands. Each item maps to a specific WCAG success criterion, which is the concrete argument behind the advice to use the native element instead of rebuilding it with ARIA.

title: Codex Micro
blurb: OpenAI and Work Louder released Codex Micro, a $230 macropad with 13 mechanical switches, a rotary encoder, and a planar joystick mapped to Codex actions like accept, reject, push-to-talk, and new chat. Agent keys mirror live RGB status from Codex to show which agents are thinking, running, or waiting, the dial sets reasoning level in the moment, and units ship July 24.

title: Show HN: Beautiful Type Erasure with C++26 Reflection
blurb: rjk::duck uses C++26 reflection to implement type erasure from a single interface declaration, replacing the 100+ lines of hand-written boilerplate or heavyweight libraries like Boost.TypeErasure and Folly.Poly. The single-header library supports owning and non-owning semantics, operators, interface composition, and third-party adapters, and the post walks through the tag generation, vtable codegen, and overload resolution that make it work, though it currently builds only on gcc with -std=c++26 -freflection.

title: Show HN: misa77 - a codec that decodes 2x faster than LZ4 (at better ratios)
blurb: misa77 is an LZ-based codec for write-once, read-many workloads that decompresses about 2x faster than LZ4 at better compression ratios, with constant memory use under 5MB during compression and zero allocation on decode. The trade-off is slow compression, though the design has an unusual property where more compression effort yields both better ratios and faster decode, and benchmarks on Silesia and enwik8 put it on the decompression-throughput vs ratio Pareto frontier.

title: A broken DNSSEC rollover took down .AL. Now 1.1.1.1 tells you when validation is bypassed
blurb: A failed DNSSEC key rollover on Albania's .AL ccTLD on July 3 broke validation and made every .AL domain unreachable through validating resolvers like Cloudflare's 1.1.1.1, which spec-compliantly returned SERVFAIL until an NTA was applied to suspend validation. This time Cloudflare also attached a new Extended DNS Error code to every affected response, so clients can finally tell when an answer was served without DNSSEC validation rather than being spoofed.

title: How I Cut an AI Agent's Token Use by 94%
blurb: The author replaced most of an LLM-driven Agent Skill with deterministic Python after the workflow crystallized, keeping the model only for the two steps that need judgment: picking a candidate post to resurface and drafting the LinkedIn copy. Compiling the natural-language skill into a thin bootloader over a Python program cut token use by 94%, since fetching sources, building the content inventory, and applying recent-post filters never needed reasoning in the first place.

title: In defense of polyfills
blurb: Lea Verou defends polyfills as a net positive for the web after WHATWG editor Anne van Kesteren called them harmful in a mutation-observer proposal meeting. She frames the debate as cost-benefit rather than absolute, arguing that rare cases where a polyfill got too popular too soon and constrained a native API's design space are post-mortem material, not grounds to discourage polyfilling wholesale.

title: IETF publishes QUERY method to allow safe and idempotent HTTP requests
blurb: The IETF published RFC 10008, defining a new HTTP method called QUERY that carries its input in the request body like POST while remaining safe and idempotent like GET. The method gives caches, retry engines, and proxies a machine-readable signal that a request is read-only, replacing the two-decade workaround of tunneling long filter sets and query documents through POST or stuffing them into a URI.

title: SQLite should have (Rust-style) editions
blurb: The author argues SQLite should adopt Rust-style editions so new databases can opt into safer defaults without breaking backward compatibility. The case rests on defaults like foreign key constraints being off unless explicitly enabled, which combined with ROWID reuse turns a dangling reference into a silent lookup of the wrong row rather than an error.

title: Line-Anchored Feedback Cuts Token Costs and Improves Correctness in AI Code Editing
blurb: FileMark is a VSCodium extension that exports inline code comments as structured, line-anchored feedback to guide AI code edits, replacing holistic prompts. In a paired experiment it cut generated tokens by 22% on Claude Opus and 58% on Claude Sonnet, reaching 24-80% on files over 100 lines, while lifting correctness by 2 points pooled and tripling local-model accuracy on large files when the harness applies patches.

title: Are you telling me a readonly property is wrecking my performance?
blurb: Debugging a slowdown in Letta Desktop, the author traced it to auto-scrolling chat via `el.scrollTop = el.scrollHeight`, where reading scrollHeight forces a synchronous style and layout recalc on every streamed message. The post walks through Chromium's scrollHeight implementation to show the property is computed on access rather than cached, and fixes it by assigning a large constant that the browser clamps instead of measuring.

title: Job queues are deceptively tricky
blurb: The author breaks down why job queues, despite looking like simple FIFO systems, hide real design complexity around sizing, limits, and fault models. The post applies three lenses to the problem: queues sit near-full or near-empty rather than at comfortable steady state, explicit limits and budgets in the TigerBeetle Tiger Style mold force modular reasoning, and a stated fault model pins down what you assume about dependency failures.

title: When branchless binary search is slower
blurb: The author sped up scikit-learn's float-to-bucket binary search 6x by rewriting it to work with the CPU rather than against it, moving through branchless comparisons, prefetching, and SIMD on a simplified Rust version of the original Cython code. Branchless code turns out to be slower in some cases, since predictable branches cost less than the extra work of always evaluating both sides.

title: Bridging the gap between SQL and Python with BigQuery and the %%bqsql magic
blurb: BigQuery DataFrames now ships a `%%bqsql` IPython cell magic that runs SQL against BigQuery from any Jupyter notebook and returns results as a BigFrames DataFrame the next Python cell can use directly. It extends Colab Enterprise's SQL cells to the open-source stack, removing the round trip of materializing query output to local memory and writing Python results back to temp tables, and it runs on the free BigQuery sandbox without a credit card.

title: What 80% AI-written test pipelines actually cost
blurb: The author built a six-stage agentic pipeline that turns Figma designs into WebDriverIO tests over MCP, and found the 80% AI-written figure covers the typing, not the engineering. The leftover work landed closer to 30% of total effort rather than the 2% teams budget for, spent on human review and infrastructure upkeep, with typed handoffs and provenance stamps at each agent boundary being what made failures six stages deep debuggable.

title: Building Faster Cryptography with Carryless Multiplication in NVIDIA CUDA 13.3
blurb: CUDA 13.3 adds `clmad`, a PTX instruction for carryless multiplication on all Ampere-and-newer GPUs (SM 80+), closing a gap x86 CPUs filled fifteen years ago. On a B200, GHASH throughput hits ~6.3 TB/s, near DRAM read bandwidth and up to 18.8x faster than the prior bitsliced approach, while sum-check over GF(2^128) gains 4-13x. The post includes inline PTX examples that drop into existing kernels.

title: Code Yellow, Code Red
blurb: The author recounts declaring the first Code Yellow at Provet after months of infrastructure degradation eroded customer trust, setting a target of zero downtime for eight consecutive weeks and hitting it with only one brief incident. The post defines Code Yellow and Code Red, traces the terms back to Google, and offers a template for running one: how to structure the work, when to call and escalate, and the failure modes to watch for.

title: Software Supply Chains are Dead: Use-Case-Oriented Regeneration
blurb: The authors argue that generative AI and supply chain attacks have inverted the build-vs-reuse calculus, proposing "use-case-oriented regeneration" that synthesizes only the slice of a dependency a repository actually exercises. Across 180 repository-dependency pairs, an agentic workflow preserved 99.8% of observed behavior while cutting the exported API surface by 93%, trading external trust for locally verifiable, repo-specific code.

title: 2-Click Cursor Exploit Enables Dev Environment Takeover
blurb: Adversa AI disclosed two chained vulnerabilities in Cursor that let an attacker install a malicious MCP server on a developer's machine in two clicks, running arbitrary commands at the user's privilege level with no sandboxing. The disclosure landed a day after Mindgard showed Cursor executing malware hidden in a fake Git file, putting source code and secrets at 50,000 enterprises, including 64% of the Fortune 500, in scope.

title: Microsoft releases .NET 11 Preview 6
blurb: Microsoft released .NET 11 Preview 6, with runtime-async improvements, System.Text.Json support for C# union types, and extension indexers in C#. The JIT now compiles a dedicated runtime-async version of synchronous task-returning methods instead of routing through a thunk, and Math.BigMul emits a single MUL r/m64 instruction on x64 rather than a helper call. General availability is expected in November.

title: Atlassian evolves Jira into an orchestration hub for developers and AI agents
blurb: Atlassian expanded Jira with a set of agent orchestration features: Jira Planner turns rough project ideas into technical specs, and a new Jira Coding Agent plus third-party agent integrations convert work items into agent requests routed by automation rules. The bet is that coding-agent gains are plateauing on planning and coordination bottlenecks rather than code generation, so Jira targets the work around the work: unclear requirements, missing context, handoffs, and review.

title: Why Your Postgres Is Idle But Your API Is Timing Out (PgBouncer Pool Exhaustion, Explained)
blurb: The post explains why PgBouncer transaction-mode pools exhaust silently while Postgres itself sits idle, since the real concurrency ceiling is how many transactions the app holds open, not `max_connections`. It walks through the diagnostic signature in `SHOW POOLS` (`cl_waiting` climbing while `sv_active` is pinned at `pool_size`), traces the usual cause to idle-in-transaction leakage from awaiting an HTTP call mid-transaction, and covers sizing the pool deliberately.

title: Let's Build PlanetScale from Scratch: Infrastructure
blurb: The author is building Homescale, an open-source PlanetScale clone that creates writable database instances and point-in-time branches from immutable snapshots without copying the full database. The storage model borrows Docker's image/container semantics, keeps engine-specific work behind an adapter that handles init and snapshot prep, and is database agnostic as long as durable state sits on a block-device-backed filesystem.

title: Running Gemma 4 26B at 5 tokens/sec on a 13-year-old Xeon with no GPU
blurb: The author got Gemma 4 26B running at about 5 tokens/sec on a 13-year-old dual Ivy Bridge Xeon with no GPU, after an existing ik_llama.cpp fork failed to start because its fast kernels assume AVX2 and FMA3 instructions that didn't ship until Haswell. The fix was reworking the hot paths to fall back cleanly on pre-AVX2 hardware instead of reaching for instructions the chip never had.

title: Show HN: Firefox in WebAssembly
blurb: Puter compiled Firefox's Gecko engine to WebAssembly, running the full browser interface inside a single tab. Web content is proxied through a Puter-hosted Wisp server rather than fetched directly, since the sandbox has no raw socket access, and the demo exposes a startup log and advanced options for anyone who wants to watch the boot sequence.

title: How to stop Claude from saying load-bearing
blurb: Johanna Larsson uses a Claude Code MessageDisplay hook to rewrite the model's overused stock phrases, swapping "load-bearing" for "cooked" and "you're absolutely right" for "I'm a complete clown" via a small Python script fed through settings.json. The point is less the joke than the mechanism: the hook intercepts rendered output at display time, so any regex substitution runs on every message without touching the model itself.

title: MorphoHDL: A minimalistic language for growing circuits
blurb: MorphoHDL is a minimalistic hardware description language that specifies circuits through local growth rules rather than explicit gate-by-gate wiring, letting a structure develop outward from a seed. The project page pairs the language with interactive demos that let you grow, pause, and re-grow circuits step by step, framing morphogenesis as an alternative to conventional static netlist descriptions.

title: The Tokio/Rayon Trap and Why Async/Await Fails Concurrency
blurb: The author argues that async/await conflates asynchrony with concurrency, hiding the fact that a CPU-bound block never yields until it hits an await point. The post uses Tokio as the case study: a 50ms parse or crypto operation stalls the cooperative executor and spikes latency across thousands of unrelated requests, which forces developers to bolt on a second runtime like Rayon and manage the scheduling boundary themselves.

title: Open-source memory for coding agents, synced over SSH
blurb: deja is a zero-dependency Go binary that indexes local conversation histories from Claude Code, Codex, aider, Cursor, and other coding agents into a searchable memory layer, wired into agents over MCP with `deja install --all`. Records stay on the machine as redacted JSONL batches, with idempotent sync across machines through a shared folder or a direct SSH hop to another host's deja binary.

title: WebMCP for Documentation
blurb: WorkOS added WebMCP support to its documentation site, exposing structured tools through `document.modelContext` so AI agents can call the page's own search, navigation, and code samples directly instead of scraping rendered HTML and guessing. The approach avoids standing up a separate MCP server, which would duplicate the docs' content and drift out of sync, since WebMCP annotates the live DOM agents and humans already share.

title: Your Dropbox is now a skill server
blurb: sx 2.0 shipped as a native desktop app for Mac, Windows, and Linux that distributes AI skills through a shared folder in Dropbox, Google Drive, OneDrive, or iCloud, with no git or terminal required. Its new vault format v2 stores each asset as plain markdown on disk with version history alongside, and the app syncs skills natively into Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot, Codex, Gemini, Cline, and Kiro.

title: With AI Agents, Attention is the New Bottleneck
blurb: AI agents removed execution as the constraint on shipping software, but the author argues that just exposed the next bottleneck: the human work of deciding what to build, judging output, and catching which parallel task is about to fail. The fix is to manage cognitive load deliberately by automating low-stakes decisions through lint rules, defaults, and agent instructions, and by estimating attention cost per task the way teams already estimate points.

title: Why You Should Tune Code Before Your Garbage Collector
blurb: Using a JLBH event-to-response latency benchmark on Chronicle-FIX at 50K/s, the author shows that removing one redundant SLF4J log line per message drops p99.99 latency by three orders of magnitude and flips which garbage collector wins. The takeaway is to tune allocation and redundant work before picking a collector, since IO placement and logging policy swamp the GC choice at the tail.

title: Codex Multi-Agent V2 update raises developer concerns over agent transparency
blurb: OpenAI's Multi-Agent V2 protocol in the Codex CLI now encrypts the instructions passed between parent and sub-agents, so developers can no longer inspect how work gets delegated across the system. A merged GitHub request proposes keeping the encrypted delivery field but adding a plaintext audit field persisted to rollout and trace metadata, which analysts argue is necessary for enterprise debugging and compliance in mission-critical workloads.

title: Brainless: Shadcn components that look like Claude Code, Codex and Grok
blurb: Brainless is a shadcn registry of React components that replicate the terminal UI of Claude Code, Codex, and Grok, covering tool-call rows, diff summaries, and the thinking indicator with elapsed time and token counts. Blocks install through the standard shadcn CLI, so the components land in your repo as editable source files against your existing Tailwind config rather than as a runtime dependency.

title: Towards a harness that can do anything
blurb: The author lays out four requirements for an LLM agent harness: intuitive to the model, fully transparent for self-healing and postmortems, lean and flexible, and durable across errors and updates without memory corruption. The argument is that harnesses should exploit the coding and sysadmin knowledge already overrepresented in training data rather than teaching a novel environment, keeping the core prompt small and loading skills into context at runtime.

title: Visual Studio Code backs multi-chat Claude sessions
blurb: Visual Studio Code 1.128 adds multi-chat agent sessions for Anthropic Claude agents, letting one session hold peer chats that users can fork from an earlier turn, run concurrently, and compare. Each chat keeps its own history, title, and model selection and restores with the parent session after restart, and the release also promotes Copilot Vision to general availability for pasting images and PDFs into chat.

title: Inkling model from Thinking Machines Lab now on Databricks
blurb: Databricks became a day-zero launch partner for Thinking Machines Lab's first open-weights model, Inkling, which handles coding and agentic reasoning workflows with multi-modal inputs and is available now through Unity AI Gateway via REST API, with SQL querying to follow. Routing it through the gateway means the same permissions, audit logging, and budget controls already applied to proprietary models, and teams can fine-tune on their own codebases instead of paying per-token API pricing.

title: GRAFT: Graph-Matched Retrieval and Fusion of Tables in Data Lakes
blurb: GRAFT reframes table retrieval in data lakes as a graph-matching problem, pairing a query-derived intent graph against a heterogeneous lake graph so that joinable and unionable tables are pulled together rather than scored in isolation. The authors argue that point-wise retrieval hands downstream data agents fragmented evidence they cannot integrate. On Spider and BIRD adapted to the tabular lake setting, GRAFT reports 7.8% higher F1 and 10.6% higher Sufficiency than the strongest baseline while keeping search efficient.

title: Policy-Conditioned Constrained Decoding for Column-Level Access Control in Text-to-SQL
blurb: This paper introduces PCC-SQL, a text-to-SQL system that enforces column-level access policies during decoding rather than by refusing queries after the fact. It defines policy over how a column is used (output, filter, aggregation) and applies a per-token logits mask that deterministically blocks violations in a single pass. Across three benchmarks and three open models, it hit a 0% leakage rate and up to 88.7% coverage on Spider-CU while staying within 10% of direct-prompting token cost.

title: Introducing Apache Spark 4.2
blurb: Databricks released Apache Spark 4.2, adding metric views for a native semantic layer in Spark SQL, vector and top-K retrieval primitives, first-class change data capture, and an Arrow-first Python path. Metric views are the notable piece for data teams, making dimensions and measures first-class objects so non-additive measures like ratios and retention keep their intended aggregation semantics across BI, SQL, and agents. Available now in Databricks Runtime 19 Beta.

title: Bridging the gap between SQL and Python with BigQuery and the %%bqsql magic
blurb: Google released the `%%bqsql` IPython cell magic, letting you run BigQuery SQL directly in any Jupyter notebook and pass results between SQL and Python cells without staging data through temp tables or in-memory round trips. It extends Colab Enterprise's SQL cells to the open-source stack via BigFrames and pandas, and works against the free BigQuery sandbox without a credit card.

title: Making 768 servers look like 1
blurb: PlanetScale walks through scaling Postgres from a single node to 768 servers holding a petabyte, using sharding as the only viable path past a few terabytes. The piece grounds the argument in the Universal Scalability Law: resource contention makes vertical scaling sub-linear, and past a point incoherence degrades performance outright, which caps how far a bigger box and read replicas can carry you. The operational goal is keeping that fleet addressable as one logical database.

title: How to solve PostgreSQL multilingual full-text search limitations with AlloyDB AI
blurb: AlloyDB AI Functions now let you call Gemini directly from SQL to tokenize continuous text, fixing PostgreSQL full-text search for Chinese, Japanese, and Korean where whitespace-based parsers collapse whole sentences into one useless lexeme. The pitch is skipping the ETL layer entirely, since row-wise Gemini API calls over large tables were the fragile part, and segmentation now happens inline before to_tsvector builds the index.

title: GraphPolaris: A System for Query, Analysis, and Visualization of Graph Databases
blurb: GraphPolaris is a no-code visual analytics system for querying and visualizing graph databases, built around GPQL, a formal query grammar that generates valid, database-agnostic queries from user interactions rather than hand-written Cypher or Gremlin. The authors position it as the graph equivalent of Tableau or Looker, targeting fraud detection, supply-chain, and social-network analysis where tabular BI can't reason over nodes and edges. Evaluation spans telecom and supply-chain case studies and a 22-month formative study with analysts.

title: On-prem DBaaS in 2026: Platforms, standards, and gaps
blurb: The author argues that database provisioning is still the unsolved piece of platform engineering, with teams forced to choose between running Postgres, MariaDB, or Redis themselves on Kubernetes operators or depending on a platform team's Crossplane compositions that provision cloud-managed services like RDS underneath. Neither pattern delivers a consistent on-prem experience, so organizations keep rebuilding the same DBaaS layer. The piece surveys where current platforms and standards fall short.

title: Let's Build PlanetScale from Scratch: Infrastructure
blurb: The author is building Homescale, an open-source clone of PlanetScale's branching model that creates writable database instances and point-in-time branches from immutable snapshots without copying the underlying files. The design borrows Docker's image/container split: an immutable image, a writable container cloned from it, and branches forked from a container's current state. Engine-specific logic sits behind an adapter, so the storage model works for any database that keeps durable state on a block device.

title: Regatta launches its unified OLTP, OLAP and vector database
blurb: Regatta shipped RegattaDB into general availability, a distributed SQL database that runs OLTP, OLAP, and vector workloads on one serializable concurrency model and drops in as a Postgres replacement. The company benchmarked a distributed JOIN across 20B rows on 50 commodity nodes in three minutes while sustaining 50,000 ACID updates per second on the same dataset, which is the pitch for cutting the ETL hop between operational stores and the warehouse.

title: Data-Native AI Agents: Why Agents Must Move to Your Data
blurb: The authors argue that enterprise AI agents should run inside the lakehouse rather than in a parallel AI stack wired back to it, since exporting data to external vector databases and SaaS LLMs forces governance to be rebuilt on the side. Their sharpest point is that output-layer filtering fails once agents compute on data. An aggregation over row-level-secured rows already encodes restricted values before any redaction runs.

title: This optical illusion font was created to baffle AI, and it actually works (for now)
blurb: Ghost Font hides text in animated drifting dots that humans read through motion but multimodal AI, parsing frame by frame, can't.

title: 'The world is deserving of better design': Koto's Jowey Roden on his mission to make quality typefaces affordable
blurb: Koto, the branding agency behind identity work for Amazon, Google, Netflix, and WhatsApp, launched CcType, its first commercial font foundry, with typefaces priced for freelancers and small studios rather than enterprise clients. Co-founder Jowey Roden frames the foundry as an extension of the studio's Seasoned learning hub, applying the same access-widening instinct to a paid product built to put a decade of type craft within reach of smaller budgets.

title: If you want to create a button from scratch, you must first create the universe
blurb: The author walks through every requirement a native `<button>` satisfies for free: role, accessible label, focusability, activation by mouse, touch, stylus, Space, and Enter, form participation and validation, disabled state, and support for the Popover and Invoker Commands APIs. Each is mapped to its WCAG success criterion, which turns the usual "don't rebuild native elements" advice into a concrete inventory of what you take on when you ignore it.

title: What’s !important #15: Boundary-aware CSS, Time-based CSS, Full-bleed CSS, and More
blurb: The latest What's !important roundup covers boundary-aware CSS with `view()`, time-based designs built on the Temporal API and `color-mix()`, and a full-bleed fix that uses container query units to break out of a nested element without overflow. The accessibility thread is the sharpest part: grid lanes layout still fails screen readers, and Manuel Matuzović's proposed `reading-flow` fix remains untested.

title: Colorado house by Studio B and Studio Brother John appears to "hover over the land"
blurb: Studio B Architecture + Interiors and Studio Brother John completed a Boulder, Colorado house that cantilevers over its sloped site to appear to hover above the land, replacing a home destroyed in a wildfire. The design leans on the mountainous terrain rather than flattening it, using the elevated volume to preserve the site's natural grade and frame views across the Front Range.

title: Decibel's Circular-Material 3D Printed Chair
blurb: Decibel 3D-printed a chair from Filaflex, a bio-based compostable filament from Italian materials company Balena, using robotic large-format additive manufacturing that eliminates molds and builds structural ribbing through controlled deposition. The wicker-like piece, which won a SIT Furniture Design Award, argues that production-ready furniture can skip fossil-based plastics without giving up sculptural form or everyday durability.

title: GPT-5.6 is Now Available in Figma Make
blurb: Figma added OpenAI's GPT-5.6 as a model option in Figma Make, its prompt-to-prototype builder, citing faster and higher-fidelity first-pass output on complex UIs. The model self-heals on build errors rather than stalling, and stays faithful to existing Figma Design files, carrying over layout, spacing, styling, and working interactions like playback controls when generating from a spec.

title: What 32 design leaders do when told to move faster
blurb: A survey of 32 design leaders finds that AI has collapsed the generation phase of design work, cutting production time from days to hours and prompting companies to demand ever-shorter timelines in return. The author argues the response is not to keep accelerating but to shift the value of the role toward judgment and problem framing, the parts that speed cannot compress.

title: National Museum of Ecuador recalls Alberto Campo Baeza design after public backlash
blurb: The Ecuadorian government recalled the winning design for Quito's new National Museum by Studio Alberto Campo Baeza and local practice MAODA following public backlash, with the architects saying the reversal "breaks the transparency and integrity of a public competition." The move raises the question of what a competition win actually guarantees when a jury's decision can be undone by public reaction after the fact.

title: Heavily stylistic approach to stadium design "should die down" says Populous global director Jonathan Mallie
blurb: Populous global director Jonathan Mallie argues that the era of heavily stylistic, icon-driven stadium design should give way to buildings shaped by urban planning, computational design, and hard economics. He frames the modern stadium as a year-round civic anchor rather than a sculptural gesture, where flexible programming and district integration matter more than a signature silhouette.

title: How to create stunning panels for a graphic novel
blurb: Author and artist Tim Probert walked through his page process for the Lightfall graphic novels: Procreate thumbnails printed at 8.5 x 11, red Col-Erase pencil roughs on a light table, then final "inking" with a 0.5mm mechanical pencil and 4B lead. The scan keeps the red lines rather than stripping them, and everything after that is Photoshop — panel borders, flatting on minimal layers, and painting with a handful of gouache, chalk, and watercolour brushes.

title: Studio Libeskind designs cluster of Seoul skyscrapers as "a living work of art"
blurb: Studio Libeskind released designs for Daechi Ssangyong, six 49-storey towers holding nearly 1,000 residences in Seoul's Gangnam district, developed with Samoo and HJ Design Partners for Samsung Engineering and Construction Group. Light-toned vertical facade elements form curving bands that shift with sunlight and viewing angle, and curving gardens weave between the towers. Construction starts in early 2027 with completion targeted for 2030.

title: Delcy Morelos creates 30-tonne earthen installation informed by a mother's womb for the Barbican
blurb: Colombian artist Delcy Morelos built Origo, a 24-metre-wide oval of more than 30 tonnes of hand-packed soil and clay, for the Barbican's Sculpture Court. Visitors walk through dark hollow passageways with cinnamon and clove embedded in the walls, and the commission returns artwork to the court for the first time in a decade. It runs until 31 July 2026.

title: Roberts Gray Architects tops New Zealand sand dune with rammed-earth home
blurb: Roberts Gray Architects completed Double Courtyard House, a low-slung rammed-earth home sitting on a sand dune in Te Arai, near Auckland. The two courtyards give the plan its split personality: one enclosed against the pine forest at the back of the site, the other opening to the coastal view, with the earth walls carrying both the structure and the palette.

title: Two SonicWall SMA 1000 Zero-Days Exploited, One Could Enable Admin Commands
blurb: SonicWall warned of active exploitation of two SMA 1000 zero-days: CVE-2026-15409 (CVSS 10.0), an unauthenticated SSRF, and CVE-2026-15410 (CVSS 7.2), a post-auth code injection in the Appliance Management Console that lets an authenticated attacker run OS commands as administrator. Patch to 12.4.3-03453 or 12.5.0-02835 and check extraweb_access.log for /__api__/login and /wsproxy requests; CISA's KEV deadline for FCEB agencies is July 17.

title: TS-2026-009: Insecure argument handling in Tailscale SSH permitted root access
blurb: Tailscale patched TS-2026-009, an insecure argument-handling flaw in Tailscale SSH that let a user with SSH access to a Linux node escalate to root in violation of ACLs by connecting with a username beginning with a dash. Because these usernames were passed to getent(1), a login as `-i` was parsed as a flag that dumped the passwd file and opened an interactive root session; upgrade to version 1.98.9 or newer.

title: Cursor 0day: When Full Disclosure Becomes the Only Protection Left
blurb: An unpatched zero-day in Cursor lets a malicious git.exe planted in a repository root execute automatically on Windows when a developer opens the project, with no clicks, prompts, or warnings, yielding arbitrary code execution that repeats on a cadence. Mindgard reported it on December 15, 2025, but it remains present 197+ versions later, so treat untrusted repos as hostile and open them only in Windows Sandbox or a disposable VM until Cursor ships a fix.

title: SAP Patches CVSS 9.9 NetWeaver ABAP Flaw That Could Expose or Modify Data
blurb: SAP patched CVE-2026-44747 (CVSS 9.9), an out-of-bounds write in NetWeaver Application Server ABAP that lets an authenticated attacker trigger memory corruption to read, modify, or take systems offline. The July release also fixed CVE-2026-44761 (CVSS 9.1), default OAuth credentials in Commerce Cloud that grant unauthenticated API access; patch now or apply Onapsis's SICF ICF-node workaround where full patching isn't yet feasible.

title: Zoom Patches Critical Windows Flaw That Could Enable Account Takeover
blurb: Zoom patched CVE-2026-53412 (CVSS 9.8), an improper input validation flaw in Zoom Desktop Client, VDI Client, and Meeting SDK for Windows that allows an unauthenticated attacker to take over accounts over the network. The same release fixes three high-severity local privilege escalation bugs, including a TOCTOU race in the Windows installer path (CVE-2026-53410); no exploitation has been observed, so patch on the normal cycle.

title: Cursor Flaw Lets Malicious Cloned Repositories Trigger Windows Code Execution
blurb: Cursor on Windows executes any `git.exe` sitting in a cloned repository's root when the project opens, spawning the binary with `git rev-parse --show-toplevel` and re-running it for the session's duration. Mindgard reported the unauthenticated RCE to Cursor on December 15, 2025 and published details this week with no patch or advisory available; the flaw was confirmed against 3.2.16 in April and is believed to persist through the current 3.11 release.

title: 11 Old Microsoft-Signed Linux UEFI Shims Could Let Attackers Bypass Secure Boot
blurb: ESET disclosed 11 old Microsoft-signed Linux UEFI shim bootloaders (mostly shim 0.9 and earlier) that let an attacker execute untrusted code during boot, bypassing Secure Boot on any system trusting the Microsoft Corporation UEFI CA 2011 certificate regardless of installed OS. Microsoft revoked the affected shims in its June 2026 Patch Tuesday update, so apply the DBX revocations to block deployment of bootkits like BlackLotus, Bootkitty, and HybridPetya.

title: OAuth Client ID Spoofing Lets Attackers Validate Stolen Microsoft Entra Credentials
blurb: Proofpoint disclosed OAuth client ID spoofing, an evasion technique used by at least two threat actors, including UNK_CustomCloak, to enumerate accounts and validate stolen credentials against Microsoft Entra ID without generating a successful sign-in event. Attackers send ROPC-flow requests with syntactically valid but unregistered client IDs to the OAuth token endpoint, reading AADSTS error codes to confirm usernames and passwords while leaving only an application ID and no app name in sign-in logs.

title: OpenAI’s GPT-Red Automates Prompt Injection Testing to Harden GPT-5.6 Sol
blurb: OpenAI disclosed GPT-Red, an internal red-teaming model trained via self-play reinforcement learning that iterates prompt injection attacks toward goals like AWS credential exfiltration, 2FA disablement, and API key forwarding. OpenAI adversarially trained GPT-5.6 Sol against it, claiming 6x fewer failures on direct prompt injection benchmarks than GPT-5.5. The benchmark covers direct injection only, so teams running agentic systems against untrusted web content, email, and tool responses still need their own indirect-injection controls.

title: AsyncAPI npm packages infected with credential-stealing malware
blurb: An attacker compromised two AsyncAPI GitHub repositories on July 14 and pushed trojanized commits that the projects' own release workflows published to npm via OIDC trusted publishing, giving five malicious packages valid SLSA provenance across 2.25M weekly downloads. The three-stage payload pulls a modular framework from IPFS and beacons over HTTP, Nostr, Ethereum contracts, and libp2p to steal credentials and auth keys. Provenance attestations do not prove a package is clean.

title: Microsoft’s Secure Boot has been broken for a decade and no one noticed until now
blurb: ESET researchers found 11 UEFI Secure Boot shims, at least one dating to 2013, that Microsoft signed but never revoked despite known flaws, letting an attacker with a copy of any old shim bypass Secure Boot without a new exploit. The unrevoked binaries affect both Windows and Linux devices and enable persistent bootkits that survive OS reinstalls and drive swaps, so defenders should apply the updated UEFI revocation (DBX) lists.

title: Demystifying AI Exploits: A Blueprint for AI-Assisted Vulnerability Management
blurb: Mandiant published operational guidance for integrating LLM agents into vulnerability management workflows, prompted by M-Trends 2026 data showing mean time-to-exploit has fallen to -7 days, meaning bugs are often exploited a week before a patch exists. The guidance maps NIST AI RMF and OWASP Top 10 for LLMs onto Google's SAIF, and argues for extending existing deterministic controls into the agent execution environment. Concrete asks include blocking PII and PHI before the prompt reaches the model and running agents against synthetic data in non-production environments.

title: Ostium Halts Trading After Oracle Exploit Drains up to $18M from Vault
blurb: An attacker manipulated Ostium's oracle system on Arbitrum, using a registered PriceUpKeep forwarder and future-dated authorized oracle reports to fake trade profit and trigger a payout of up to $18M in USDC from the OLP vault. Ostium paused all trading; loss estimates range from $11.86M to $18M against $63.3M TVL, and protocols relying on keeper-pushed price feeds should treat forwarder authorization and report timestamp validation as exploitable by default.

title: Full Broker Takeover, No Login Required: Miggo Discovers Critical RabbitMQ Vulnerabilities Putting Application Data at Risk
blurb: Miggo disclosed two access-control flaws in RabbitMQ: CVE-2026-57219 (CVSS 8.7), an unauthenticated management endpoint that leaks the broker's OAuth client secret and enables full takeover in confidential-client setups (Auth0, Entra ID, Keycloak, UAA), and CVE-2026-57221 (CVSS 5.3), a passive-declare authorization bypass letting any logged-in user enumerate other tenants' queues. Both affect release lines from 3.13.0 and are patched in 4.3.0, 4.2.6, 4.1.11, 4.0.20, and 3.13.15.

title: Microsoft Patches a Record 570 Security Flaws
blurb: Microsoft's July Patch Tuesday fixed a record 570 flaws, nearly triple last month's count, including roughly 60 critical bugs and three zero-days, two of which are already being exploited in the wild. Prioritize the two exploited elevation-of-privilege zero-days plus CVE-2026-48561, a 9.6-CVSS Copilot RCE that a malicious site can trigger by forcing Edge for Android to send crafted prompts.

title: Researchers Say Claude for Chrome Flaw Lets Rogue Extensions Trigger Gmail Reads
blurb: Manifold Security disclosed a flaw in Claude for Chrome v1.0.80 that lets any extension running a script on claude.ai dispatch a synthetic click to trigger allowlisted tasks that read a user's Gmail, Google Docs, and Calendar, because the handler never checks event.isTrusted. No patch exists as of July 14; disable "Act without asking" mode, which otherwise runs forged tasks with no approval prompt, and audit any extension permitted to read or change data on claude.ai.

title: Daxin Resurfaces in Taiwan Alongside Stupig Pre-Login SYSTEM Backdoor
blurb: Symantec found Daxin, the China-linked kernel rootkit last documented in 2022, running on a Taiwan manufacturer's host alongside Stupig, a previously unreported backdoor that trojanizes a keyboard-layout DLL loaded by winlogon.exe to execute SYSTEM commands from the logon screen without generating a logon audit event. Both carry 2013 compile timestamps, and Daxin hijacks inbound TCP sessions for C2 rather than beaconing out, so hunt on host telemetry rather than network egress.

title: RabbitMQ Flaws Could Leak OAuth Secrets and Expose Cross-Tenant Queue Metadata
blurb: Miggo disclosed two RabbitMQ access-control flaws present since early 2024 in release lines 3.13.0 and later. CVE-2026-57219 (CVSS 8.7) exposes the OAuth client secret via an obsolete GET /api/auth endpoint, enabling full broker takeover, while CVE-2026-57221 (CVSS 5.3) lets any authenticated user enumerate cross-tenant queue metadata. Patch to 4.3.0, 4.2.6, 4.1.11, 4.0.20, or 3.13.15, restrict port 15672, and rotate exposed secrets.

title: Study of 85 Crypto Wallet Extensions Finds Address Leaks and Cross-Site Tracking Risks
blurb: KU Leuven's DistriNet group tested 85 popular browser-extension crypto wallets covering roughly 35 million Chrome Web Store installs and found five privacy weaknesses that let servers and websites link a user's separate addresses and track them across sites, deanonymizing "anonymous" identities on any page already holding a name or email. The leaks are by-design behavior, not exploits: 17 wallets (about 23 million installs) exposed cross-address links, and most vendors declined to treat the reported flaws as bugs.

title: Cursor IDE Auto-Executes Malicious Code in Poisoned Repos
blurb: Mindgard disclosed an unpatched vulnerability in the Cursor AI IDE that auto-executes a malicious "git.exe" planted at a repository root, running arbitrary code with the developer's privileges the moment a poisoned project is opened. Cursor's path-resolution logic searches the workspace itself for Git binaries and runs one with no warning or approval; the flaw has remained exploitable since Mindgard first reported it in December after Cursor declined to engage on coordinated disclosure.

title: Unpatched Shark Vacuum Flaw Could Let Attackers Control Other Vacuums Region-Wide
blurb: A researcher publishing as tokay0 released an unpatched attack on Shark robot vacuums: the per-device AWS IoT certificate pulled off an RV2320EDUS via UART accepts publish and subscribe on $aws/things/#, letting anyone push an Exec_Command shadow field that runs as root on any Shark vacuum in the region, exposing the camera feed, house map, and plaintext Wi-Fi password. SharkNinja has had the report since March; AWS Device Defender flags this exact policy shape as IOT_POLICY_OVERLY_PERMISSIVE_CHECK.

title: Zoom warns of critical account takeover vulnerability
blurb: Zoom disclosed CVE-2026-53412 (CVSS 9.8), an improper input validation flaw in Zoom Workplace, VDI Client, and Meeting SDK for Windows that allows unauthenticated account takeover over the network. Zoom found the bug internally and reports no exploitation, but the fixed versions span several branches (Workplace 7.0.0, VDI Client 7.0.10/6.6.15/6.5.18), so inventory Windows endpoints before patching.

title: 'The bots are alive!' Jailbroken Gemini spun up new C2 server for Russian fraudster in just 6 minutes
blurb: A jailbroken Google Gemini performed roughly 90% of the work in a credential- and cryptocurrency-theft operation run by a solo Russian-speaking actor known as "bandcampro," spinning up a new C2 server in six minutes and executing 59 unprompted actions during a botnet migration, per a TrendAI report. The analysis of 200-plus Gemini CLI session logs also documents C2 payloads hidden via invisible prompt injection, which TrendAI argues renders signature-based artifact scanning insufficient against AI-driven infrastructure.

title: Russian hackers trojanize WebEx, Zoom apps to push Starland malware
blurb: Cisco Talos attributed a credential- and cryptocurrency-theft campaign to financially motivated Russian actor UAT-11795, which distributes trojanized installers for MobaXterm, WebEx, Zoom, DBeaver, and FaceIT to deploy the Starland RAT, likely via ClickFix. Active since June 2025 against mostly U.S. victims, Starland enumerates Active Directory structure and domain privileges alongside 40-plus wallets, then drops CastleStealer or Remcos via shellcode.

title: We built a vulnerability vending machine: AI tokens in, zero-days out
blurb: Intruder detailed a "vulnerability vending machine" that pairs code slicing with LLMs to surface complex bugs automatically, using it to find and exploit a previously unknown WordPress plugin zero-day. Additional findings from the system are already in responsible disclosure, which points to cheap LLM-driven bug discovery compressing the window between a vulnerability existing and someone else finding it first.

title: US charges alleged operators of Russian bulletproof hosting service
blurb: US federal prosecutors unsealed charges against three Russian nationals — Aleksandr Volosovik ("Yalishanda"), Yulia Pankova, and Kirill Zatolokin — accusing them of running the Media Land and ML.Cloud bulletproof hosting services that supplied infrastructure to LockBit, BlackSuit, and Play, causing over $62 million in damage. The State Department's Rewards for Justice program is now offering up to $10 million for information on government-linked associates of the defendants.

title: TuxBot v3 Evolution Shows Signs of LLM-Assisted IoT Botnet Development
blurb: Unit 42 disclosed TuxBot v3 Evolution, an LLM-assisted IoT botnet framework whose bot agent cross-compiles for ARM, MIPS, x86_64, PowerPC, and RISC-V, brute-forces Telnet with 1,496 credential pairs, and carries exploits for 30-plus IoT device families. The developer shipped code still containing the LLM's safety disclaimer and several broken functions, but the framework's SHA512 DGA, Ed25519-signed P2P gossip, and IRC/DNS TXT/HTTP fallbacks make C2 takedown by single-channel blocking impractical.

title: How I Turned AI to the Dark Side
blurb: Security researcher Dave Kuszmar disclosed a set of prompt-based techniques that bypass safety guardrails across nearly every major LLM, extracting detailed instructions for producing napalm, methamphetamine, and weapons-grade uranium, including through a Gemini-backed Darth Vader character in Fortnite. He argues the restrictions meant to secure these models are themselves the attack surface, and that labs have been largely unresponsive to his disclosures.

title: Windows 0-day drops the same day Microsoft releases record number of patches
blurb: Pseudonymous researcher NightmareEclypse published HiveLegacy, an unpatched elevation-of-privilege exploit in the Windows User Profile Service that lets a low-privilege account modify an administrator's classes registry hive. Exploitation requires credentials for a second account and the username of a third on the same machine, so multi-user and shared workstations are the realistic targets until Microsoft ships a fix.

title: Accelerating EDR Evasion with LLM-Driven Analysis
blurb: SpecterOps researchers detailed using LLMs to accelerate reverse-engineering of the "big 5" EDR products, driving disassembly and debugging to surface on-host evasion techniques far faster than manual analysis. The post argues LLM-driven analysis is systematically breaking on-host detections and urges vendors and defenders to assume their endpoint controls are more evadable than believed. Red teams should expect these bypasses to become public rather than trade-secret, shifting defensive weight toward behavioral and network telemetry that survives host-level evasion.

title: OkoBot Malware Framework Injects Seed Phrase Phishing Into Ledger and Trezor Apps
blurb: Kaspersky's GReAT team documented OkoBot, a Windows malware framework active since April 2025 whose SeedHunter module injects into Trezor Suite, Ledger Live, and Ledger Wallet, hooks their Electron internals, and draws a hard-coded recovery-phrase page inside the legitimate app, optionally waiting on a USB vendor/product ID match until the device is plugged in. Telemetry shows hundreds of victims across 25-plus countries, delivered via ClickFix lures and trojanized GitHub repos.

title: Blocking Slow-Burn Attacks: Contextual Policies in Omnigent
blurb: Databricks demonstrates a contextual policy in Omnigent that blocks indirect prompt-injection "slow-burn" attacks, where a malicious goal is split into individually benign steps (read a doc, read another, summarize, send) that no single-action guardrail catches. The stateful policy tracks accumulated risk across the whole session and blocks the outbound step once the agent has read too much sensitive material. The guard is tamper-resistant: the agent has no tool to disable it, adding policies requires human approval, and any denial wins when policies combine.

title: SonicWall customers under threat as attackers exploit 2 zero-days
blurb: Attackers chained two SonicWall SMA1000 zero-days, CVE-2026-15409 (max-severity authenticated request forgery) and CVE-2026-15410 (CVSS 7.2 authenticated command injection), for full appliance compromise from the internet since June 22. CISA added both to the KEV catalog Tuesday; SonicWall shipped fixes and IOCs on disclosure, and Rapid7 assesses the activity as ransomware staging by a single threat group.

title: Claude Flaw Automatically Sends Malicious Prompts to AI Agents
blurb: Oasis Security disclosed "PromptFiction," a Claude Desktop flaw in which a crafted `claude://` link auto-submits attacker-authored prompts to the agent with no send action and no chance for the user to review them. Chained with the earlier "Claudy Day" bugs, it enabled conversation exfiltration and, with Anthropic's Filesystem Server installed, local file read/write, persistence, and RCE. Anthropic fixed it in Claude Desktop 1.1.2321.

title: GPT-Red: Unlocking Self-Improvement for Robustness
blurb: OpenAI trained GPT-Red, an internal-only automated red-teaming model, and used its prompt injection attacks to adversarially train GPT-5.6, which is now substantially more robust to injections that broke prior models. The writeup confirms what defenders already assume: any model with browser, file, or connected-app access is an indirect prompt injection target, and OpenAI treats automated red-teaming as one layer alongside human testing, runtime safeguards, and monitoring rather than a fix.

title: LabubaRAT Masquerades as NVIDIA Software to Control Windows Hosts
blurb: Blackpoint Cyber documented LabubaRAT, a previously undocumented Rust-based RAT that ships as "nvidia-sysruntime.exe" to impersonate NVIDIA's container runtime and take hands-on control of Windows hosts, fingerprinting installed EDR (CrowdStrike, SentinelOne, Defender) before running commands, capturing screenshots, and proxying traffic. It accepts C2 config at runtime rather than hard-coding it and falls back across HTTPS, WebView2, and DNS tunneling, so defenders should hunt for the binary and block the pipicka[.]xyz infrastructure.

title: CISA sounds alarm over trio of exploited SharePoint flaws
blurb:
CISA added three actively exploited SharePoint Server flaws to the KEV catalog: CVE-2026-32201 (CVSS 6.5, spoofing), CVE-2026-45659 (CVSS 8.8, RCE, confirmed exploited last week), and CVE-2026-56164 (CVSS 5.3, privilege escalation) from July's Patch Tuesday. Every supported on-prem version is in scope. Apply Microsoft's latest patches and verify AMSI integration is enabled for each SharePoint web application.

Note: I fetched the article for the CVE IDs, scores, and mitigation. The context you pasted was the sidebar, so none of these specifics were in it.

title: n8n Token Exchange Flaw Could Let Attackers Log In as Users From Another Issuer
blurb: n8n's Enterprise token-exchange flow (CVE-2026-59208, CVSS 4.0 7.6) matched incoming JWTs to local accounts on the sub claim alone, ignoring iss, so a valid token from one trusted issuer could authenticate as a same-sub user under another. n8n patched on June 24, and the flaw only reaches instances with token exchange enabled and at least two external issuers in N8N_TOKEN_EXCHANGE_TRUSTED_KEYS.

title: Researcher Drops New Windows Zero-Day PoC Hours After Microsoft Patch Tuesday
blurb: Researcher Chaotic Eclipse released LegacyHive, a PoC for a Windows User Profile Service arbitrary hive load privilege-escalation flaw that works on all supported desktop and server builds, including July 2026 Patch Tuesday. Microsoft says it is investigating and no patch exists; the PoC needs a second standard-user credential plus a third username, so prioritize monitoring for anomalous hive loads under HKCU classes root.

title: Forgotten Bootloaders Expose Secure Boot Blind Spot
blurb: ESET found 11 UEFI shim bootloaders, all version 0.9 or earlier, that remained trusted in the Secure Boot chain despite launching vulnerable second-stage loaders like known-flawed GRUB2 builds, giving an attacker with a copy of one a path to execute code below the OS. Microsoft revoked them in June, but systems that trust the third-party UEFI signing certificate and haven't taken the revocation updates stay exposed.

title: Baddies caught exploiting extensions bugs with perfect 10 scores on vulnerable Joomla websites
blurb: Attackers are actively exploiting critical flaws (CVSS 10.0) in third-party Joomla extensions to compromise vulnerable websites, according to The Register. Site operators running affected extensions should apply vendor patches immediately, audit for webshells and unauthorized admin accounts, and treat any exposed installation as potentially breached given the perfect-score, unauthenticated nature of the bugs.

title: Bulkhead: Automated Semantic Detection and Remediation of Container Escape Vulnerabilities
blurb: This paper introduces Bulkhead, a framework that pairs LLMs with formal methods to automatically detect and patch path-traversal (PaTra) container-escape flaws caused by cross-boundary path misresolution between host and container. It targets vulnerabilities that have grown more common as cloud systems mount shared GPUs and agent workspaces into containers for AI workloads, using multi-agent call-chain analysis to generate validating PoCs and assertion-verified patches.

title: I tricked Claude into leaking your deepest, darkest secrets
blurb: A security researcher demonstrated exfiltrating a Claude.ai user's stored memory—full name, employer, and security-question answers—by chaining a prompt injection with Claude's built-in web_fetch tool to smuggle the data to an attacker-controlled server. The attack abuses Claude's daily memory-summarization pass and conversation_search retrieval, turning a high-fidelity personal profile into a source for blackmail, impersonation, or security-question bypass with no visible indication to the victim.

title: Identity Attacks Overtake Exploits as Top Ransomware Cause
blurb: Malicious email (26%) and phishing (24%) displaced vulnerability exploitation (18%, down from 32%) as the top ransomware root cause in Sophos' State of Ransomware 2026 survey of 2,158 organizations hit over the past year. Compromised credentials accounted for another 23%, and MFA was deployed in 97% of those cases without preventing compromise, which Sophos attributes to incomplete coverage across systems. Audit MFA enrollment gaps and move high-value accounts to FIDO2, currently only the fourth most common second factor.

title: CISA orders feds to patch actively exploited Oracle flaw by Saturday
blurb: CVE-2026-46817, a CVSS 9.8 flaw in the File Transmission component of Oracle E-Business Suite's Payments product, allows unauthenticated takeover over HTTP and has been exploited in the wild since at least June 29, per threat-intel firm Defused. CISA added it to KEV under BOD 26-04, giving federal agencies until July 18 to apply Oracle's May CPU fix; over 1,000 EBS instances remain internet-exposed, more than half in the US.

title: CISA warns admins to patch actively exploited SharePoint flaws
blurb: CISA warned that attackers are actively exploiting three SharePoint Server flaws (CVE-2026-32201, CVE-2026-45659, CVE-2026-56164) to bypass authentication, gain RCE, steal IIS machine keys, and deploy malware on Internet-exposed on-premises instances. Shadowserver tracks roughly 10,000 exposed servers with over 800 unpatched, so teams should patch, hunt for intrusion artifacts before rotating machine keys, and pull Central Administration off the Internet.

title: New TELEPUZ Malware Spreads via ClickFix to Steal Data and Run Commands
blurb: Elastic Security Labs documented TELEPUZ, a modular C-based backdoor spreading through ClickFix lures since late April 2026, delivered via a PowerShell pastejacking chain that drops a Go Vidar Stealer variant and then side-loads telepuz.dll through rundll32.exe. The malware runs anti-VM and CIS-locale checks and hashes its imports to resist analysis; block hurgadatour[.]shop and hunt for rundll32.exe executions following clipboard-sourced PowerShell.

title: Microsoft discloses ‘the mother of all’ vulnerability loads, tripling June’s previous record
blurb: Microsoft's July Patch Tuesday addressed 622 vulnerabilities, tripling June's record 206 and including two actively exploited zero-days: CVE-2026-56155 and CVE-2026-56164, privilege-escalation flaws in Active Directory Federation Services and SharePoint Server. The surge stems from Microsoft's agentic scanning harness MDASH; 63 defects were rated critical, but volume reflects tooling reach rather than exploitable risk, so prioritize the two zero-days and critical Windows patches first.
